proposals.title: A Control-Oriented, Friction-Aware Design Framework with Relaxed Auxiliary Error Constraints for Robust Performance
proposals.motivation: Many robust and adaptive control designs, including those based on barrier Lyapunov functions or prescribed performance, rely on a specific, uniform formulation of an auxiliary tracking error to guarantee stability and performance bounds. This assumption can be overly restrictive, limiting controller design flexibility and complicating the integration of realistic, learned nonlinearities like friction. Pre-2026 advances in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for friction identification and control-oriented stochastic optimization create an opportunity to revisit this core constraint.
proposals.hypothesis: By decoupling the stability proof from a specific auxiliary error definition, a more flexible controller architecture can be synthesized that directly incorporates identified, non-parametric friction models while maintaining global boundedness and achieving quantifiable performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic optimization framework for control-oriented identification, using PINN-derived LuGre-type friction models as prior knowledge. The core control design will employ a sampling-based policy optimization method (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method or model-based reinforcement learning) to search over a broad class of stabilizing controllers not requiring a pre-defined auxiliary error transformation. Stability will be enforced via a probabilistic verification step, integrating recent results on probably approximately correct (PAC) bounds for learned systems, ensuring robust performance despite the relaxed structural assumption.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic design methodology for nonlinear control that is less dependent on specific error variable transformations, increasing design freedom. It will demonstrate a principled integration of high-fidelity, data-driven friction models into a stability-guaranteed control loop, advancing the practical application of learning in control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally relaxing the uniform auxiliary error assumption—a common but rarely questioned construct—and replacing it with a data-driven, optimization-based synthesis that directly accommodates complex identified dynamics, moving beyond proof techniques that mandate a specific error formulation.

proposals.title: Adaptive Sliding Mode Control with Online Parameter Estimation via Stochastic Optimization for Systems with Friction
proposals.motivation: Sliding mode control (SMC) offers robust performance against matched uncertainties but can suffer from high-gain chattering, especially when parametric uncertainties are large. Pre-2026, a key challenge in control systems, particularly in precision mechatronics and biomedical devices, is maintaining performance in the presence of time-varying or poorly known parameters, such as friction coefficients or drug transport rates. Combining robust control with online adaptation can reduce conservatism and improve transient response.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a continuous-time recursive parameter estimator, based on stochastic optimization principles, directly into the SMC design will yield an adaptive law that guarantees asymptotic tracking and parameter convergence under persistent excitation, while significantly attenuating chattering compared to standard high-gain SMC.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework where a Lyapunov-based sliding surface is designed concurrently with a parameter update law derived from a stochastic gradient descent algorithm applied to a filtered prediction error. The method will use online sampling of the system state to perform real-time estimation of LuGre friction or similar model parameters. The adaptive law will be rigorously embedded into the control signal to continuously modulate the switching gain, ensuring it is just sufficient to overcome the estimated uncertainty bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified design methodology for adaptive robust control that explicitly links estimation quality to control performance. It will deliver a provably stable algorithm, validated in simulation and on a friction-dominated experimental testbed, demonstrating reduced control effort and improved tracking accuracy. The results will advance the co-design of estimation and control for uncertain nonlinear systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct synthesis of a stochastic optimization-based online estimator—inspired by Hespanha's recent work on control-oriented identification—with the sliding mode control structure, creating a single, cohesive adaptive robust controller rather than a simple cascade of separate estimation and control modules.

proposals.title: Constraint-Aware 3D Trajectory Tracking via Stochastic Model Predictive Control with Friction-Aware Identification
proposals.motivation: High-performance 3D trajectory tracking for autonomous vehicles or robotic manipulators is fundamentally limited by actuator saturation, velocity bounds, and complex friction dynamics. Explicitly handling these state and input constraints is critical for safety and performance but remains challenging for real-time control, especially when system models, particularly friction, are uncertain. Model Predictive Control (MPC) is a natural framework for constraint handling, but its efficacy depends on accurate, computationally efficient models and optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a control-oriented, data-driven friction identification method (like a refined LuGre model) into a stochastic MPC framework will enable robust, real-time 3D trajectory tracking while explicitly and reliably satisfying all operational constraints, outperforming standard constrained linear controllers.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage approach. First, we will employ a stochastic optimization-based identification method, informed by Hespanha's recent work on Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for LuGre models, to learn a compact, accurate friction model from experimental data. Second, this identified model will be embedded within a stochastic MPC (SMPC) formulation. The online SMPC will solve a finite-horizon optimal control problem at each sampling instant using a fast numerical solver (e.g., based on real-time iteration or ADMM), explicitly incorporating the learned friction dynamics and hard constraints on inputs and states to compute the optimal control sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified methodology for constraint-aware 3D tracking that tightly integrates advanced system identification with modern predictive control. It will provide a benchmark for performance versus constraint satisfaction in nonlinear systems with friction. The resulting framework will be validated in high-fidelity simulation for a multi-rotor UAV or robotic arm, demonstrating improved tracking precision and robustness compared to constrained PID or linear MPC.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific synthesis of a stochastic, control-oriented friction identification process (a core theme from Hespanha's recent papers) with a tailored SMPC for 3D tracking, creating a cohesive design loop from data to constrained control. This moves beyond applying generic MPC to a problem where the model fidelity is co-designed for the control objective.

proposals.title: Inverse Trajectory Generation for Underactuated Systems via Control-Oriented Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Learning
proposals.motivation: For underactuated systems, designing controllers that respect inherent dynamic constraints is a well-studied problem. However, the inverse problem—generating dynamically feasible reference trajectories that are compatible with a specific, proposed underactuated controller's capabilities—remains a significant bottleneck for deployment in complex environments like robotics or aerospace. Current methods often decouple trajectory planning from controller design, leading to infeasible commands and degraded performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding the structure and constraints of a proposed underactuated controller directly into a stochastic optimization framework, we can generate reference trajectories that are provably feasible for that controller, thereby bridging the gap between planning and execution.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse trajectory generation as a constrained stochastic optimization problem, leveraging Hespanha's expertise in control-oriented identification. The proposed controller's dynamics and actuation limits will be encoded as hard constraints. We will employ a sampling-based optimization algorithm (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method or Model Predictive Path Integral control) to explore the space of feasible trajectories. To improve efficiency for systems with complex friction or contact, we will integrate pre-trained, physics-informed neural network models (as in Hespanha's prior work) as surrogate dynamic constraints within the optimizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, controller-aware framework for generating feasible trajectories, directly enhancing the performance and reliability of underactuated systems. It will yield a new algorithmic tool for co-designing plans and controls, with applications in robotic manipulation and autonomous vehicles where actuator limits are critical.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct inversion of the controller design problem to synthesize trajectories, moving beyond ad-hoc motion planning or iterative simulation-tuning loops. It uniquely combines stochastic optimization with embedded, learned physics models specifically for this inverse feasibility task.

proposals.title: Impulsive Input-to-State Stability for Networked Control Systems: A Framework for Co-Design of Scheduling and Control
proposals.motivation: Networked control systems (NCS) are fundamental to modern infrastructure but suffer from bandwidth constraints and packet dropouts, degrading performance and stability. Pre-2026, tools for analyzing such systems under event-triggered or impulsive communication are often application-specific, lacking a unified framework for co-designing communication protocols and controllers with formal guarantees. Extending the impulsive ISS framework developed for remote estimation provides a principled path to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: The impulsive ISS framework can be generalized to provide a unified stability analysis and co-design methodology for a broad class of NCS, where the communication schedule and control law are jointly optimized to ensure robustness against network imperfections.
proposals.method: We will formulate the NCS as an impulsive dynamical system where state resets model information updates via the network. The core method involves deriving new vector Lyapunov function conditions for impulsive ISS, integrating tools from Hespanha's prior work on stochastic optimization and control-oriented identification. We will develop a co-design algorithm that uses these conditions as constraints in a stochastic optimization problem, sampling over network disturbance realizations (e.g., delays, dropouts) to synthesize a provably stable, performance-optimal pair of event-triggering policy and feedback controller.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a general, verifiable framework for the stability analysis of NCS under aperiodic communication. It will provide a concrete computational tool for the joint optimization of control and network scheduling policies, advancing the field beyond isolated design. The results will be validated in simulation on multi-agent consensus and platooning problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic extension of the impulsive ISS formalism from estimation to general feedback control, and the explicit integration of this analysis into a stochastic co-design optimization loop, which is not present in prior NCS literature.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Estimation and Optimal Policy Synthesis for Systems with Drifting Physical Parameters
proposals.motivation: High-performance control and estimation algorithms, from friction compensation in robotics to drug delivery in pharmacokinetics, rely on accurate, time-invariant system models. However, physical parameters like friction coefficients or drug transport rates can drift significantly due to wear, environmental changes, or physiological adaptation, degrading performance and safety. A systematic framework for the joint identification and control of systems with explicit, non-stationary parameter dynamics is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling parameter drift as a stochastic process and embedding this model within a control-oriented identification and policy optimization loop will yield robust controllers that significantly outperform those designed for time-invariant systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage stochastic optimization framework. First, we will perform control-oriented identification using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to learn a base model and a stochastic differential equation governing parameter drift from historical data (e.g., from seconds-resolved pharmacokinetic measurements). Second, we will formulate a finite-horizon optimal control problem where the parameter's future evolution is a disturbance with known stochastic properties, solved via scenario-based sampling techniques (e.g., Model Predictive Control with particle-based uncertainty propagation) to synthesize adaptive policies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous methodology for the design of adaptive controllers for systems with drifting physics, directly linking identification of drift dynamics to robust policy synthesis. It will deliver concrete algorithms and benchmark their performance against classical adaptive and robust control in case studies from robotics (friction) and biomedical engineering (drug transport).
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, control-oriented treatment of parameter drift as an explicit stochastic process, moving beyond simple online parameter estimation or worst-case robust control. This combines pre-2026 advances in PINNs for physics-based identification with stochastic optimization for control under non-stationarity.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Observer Synthesis via Physics-Informed Stochastic Optimization for Systems with Friction
proposals.motivation: Full-state feedback is a standard but often unrealistic assumption in control design, especially for complex mechanical and biomedical systems where only partial, noisy outputs are measurable. This gap is critical in applications like precision motion control and targeted drug delivery, where unmodeled dynamics like friction or transport delays can severely degrade performance. A unified framework for co-designing estimation and control under realistic sensing constraints is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A control law synthesized jointly with a physics-informed observer via stochastic optimization will achieve performance comparable to full-state feedback, even when only noisy, partial output measurements are available and the system contains hard-to-model nonlinearities like friction.
proposals.method: We will formulate a stochastic optimal control problem where the cost is the expected value of a performance metric over system and measurement noise, with decision variables being both the controller and observer parameters. The method leverages Hespanha's expertise in control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization. The observer will be a hybrid model combining a LuGre friction model identified via Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with a stochastic filter, ensuring physical consistency. Optimization will be performed using gradient-based methods on sampled trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, practical methodology for output-feedback control design for systems with complex internal dynamics, directly linking identification and control. It will yield verifiable performance bounds for the output-feedback case relative to the full-state ideal. The framework will be validated on high-precision motion control and compartmental drug transport models.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond the sequential 'identify-then-control' paradigm by performing joint observer-controller synthesis via stochastic optimization. It uniquely integrates physics-informed friction models directly into the observer structure within this co-design framework, rather than treating them as a separate identification step.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Converse Lyapunov Functions for Nonlinear and Time-Varying Systems via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Learning
proposals.motivation: Classical Lyapunov stability theory for nonlinear systems often relies on analytically constructing a suitable Lyapunov function, which is intractable for complex, data-driven, or time-varying models. This limitation hinders the rigorous certification of stability and performance in modern applications like robotic systems with friction or pharmacokinetic models with time-varying transport rates. A framework to algorithmically construct or approximate Lyapunov functions from data or uncertain models is a critical open problem in control theory.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating stochastic optimization for control-oriented identification with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), one can algorithmically synthesize accurate Lyapunov function candidates and stability certificates for a broad class of nonlinear and time-varying systems where analytical methods fail.
proposals.method: The approach merges Hespanha's recent methodological threads. First, we will use stochastic optimization (as in 'Control-Oriented Identification via Stochastic Optimization') to identify parsimonious, control-relevant nonlinear dynamics from data. For the identified or given model, we will train a neural network to represent a Lyapunov function candidate. Its training loss will be a physics-informed objective, penalizing violations of Lyapunov conditions (positive definiteness, negative definite derivative) across a domain sampled via adaptive sampling techniques, directly extending the PINN-based friction identification framework. Time-varying systems will be addressed by parameterizing the Lyapunov function and its derivative with respect to both state and time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, computational tool for stability analysis of systems where traditional Lyapunov theory is inapplicable. It will deliver new sufficient conditions for stability expressed through the learnability of a Lyapunov network, extending rigorous analysis to complex models in robotics (friction) and biomedicine (drug transport).
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis lies in using control-oriented stochastic identification to obtain a tractable model representation, followed by a PINN-style formulation where the network's primary 'physics' constraint is the mathematical definition of a Lyapunov function, creating a direct, data-to-certificate pipeline.

proposals.title: Robust Control Synthesis via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Uncertainty Propagation
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from biomedical drug delivery to robotic manipulation, must operate reliably despite significant model uncertainties and disturbances. Pre-2026 methods for robust control, such as H-infinity synthesis, can be conservative and computationally intensive, while data-driven approaches often lack formal stability guarantees. This gap limits the performance and applicability of controllers in safety-critical domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A control design framework that integrates stochastic optimization for parameter identification with a physics-informed propagation of uncertainty bounds will yield controllers with less conservative, yet provably robust, stability guarantees for uncertain nonlinear systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, control-oriented identification and synthesis pipeline. First, we will employ the stochastic optimization techniques from Hespanha's prior work to identify a nominal LuGre-type friction model and its parameter uncertainty distribution from data. Second, we will formulate a constrained stochastic optimal control problem where the controller must stabilize a family of systems defined by sampling from these uncertainty distributions. The optimization will use a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture, not for direct identification, but as a differentiable simulator to efficiently propagate uncertainty and evaluate stability constraints during gradient-based controller synthesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical methodology for synthesizing robust nonlinear controllers with quantifiable stability margins. It will advance the field by providing a rigorous link between data-driven uncertainty quantification and control design, moving beyond worst-case analysis. The resulting framework will be validated on high-precision mechatronic and pharmacological transport systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of identification and control via a shared stochastic optimization, where a PINN is repurposed for efficient uncertainty propagation within the synthesis loop, rather than solely for model fitting. This creates a direct, performance-oriented path from data to a certifiably robust controller.

proposals.title: Optimal Stochastic Observer Synthesis via Physics-Informed Neural Networks for High-Noise Dynamical Systems
proposals.motivation: High-fidelity state estimation in systems with significant sensor noise remains a critical bottleneck for control performance, particularly in fields like biomedical engineering (e.g., drug transport monitoring) and robotics (e.g., friction identification). Traditional Kalman-based observers often fail when noise is non-Gaussian or when system dynamics are highly nonlinear and partially unknown. A control-oriented, optimal framework for observer design under these conditions is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with stochastic optimization for observer synthesis will yield estimators that are provably more robust to significant, structured sensor noise than conventional model-based or purely data-driven approaches.
proposals.method: We will formulate observer design as a stochastic optimization problem, directly minimizing a control-oriented cost function (e.g., expected estimation error) over the parameters of a neural network-based observer. The PINN architecture will embed known physical laws (e.g., LuGre friction dynamics or pharmacokinetic models) as soft constraints, regularizing learning. We will employ adaptive sampling techniques to efficiently explore the state-noise space and use tools from stochastic approximation theory to guarantee convergence properties of the training algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, optimal design methodology for nonlinear observers in high-noise environments, directly linking estimation performance to control objectives. It will deliver a software framework and demonstrate quantifiable performance gains in two case studies: seconds-resolved drug concentration estimation and precise friction state identification for robotic control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of the observer's structure (via physics-informed neural networks) and its optimal tuning through a direct stochastic optimization of a control-relevant cost, moving beyond the separation principle and hand-tuned gain schedules prevalent in pre-2026 practice.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Linear System Identification via Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Stochastic Optimization
proposals.motivation: Accurate linear models are fundamental for high-performance control design, yet identifying them from complex, noisy, and high-dimensional data remains challenging. Traditional subspace and prediction error methods can struggle with nonlinear measurement artifacts or when data is limited, as seen in applications like precision mechatronics or biomedical systems. This work addresses the need for robust, data-efficient identification methods that yield models directly suitable for controller synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid architecture combining a linear state-space core with physics-informed neural network (PINN) encoders for input/output processing can learn more accurate and control-relevant linear models from complex operational data than conventional black-box neural networks or purely classical methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a structured neural network where the internal dynamics are constrained to a linear, parameterized state-space model (A,B,C,D). The network's encoder and decoder layers will be PINNs, regularized by known physical laws (e.g., conservation constraints) or actuator/sensor characteristics, to pre-process inputs and outputs. This architecture will be trained end-to-end using a stochastic optimization framework (e.g., Adam with mini-batch sampling) to minimize a multi-part loss function combining prediction error, model simplicity (e.g., nuclear norm on A), and physical consistency penalties, directly aligning with control-oriented metrics.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel system identification tool that bridges the interpretability and control-design readiness of classical linear methods with the flexibility of deep learning. It will provide a methodological framework for injecting partial physical knowledge into neural identification, improving data efficiency and model reliability. The outcome will be directly applicable to problems in friction compensation and drug transport modeling, where linear approximations are valuable but hard to extract from rich datasets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the enforced architectural separation of linear dynamics from nonlinear pre-processing via physics-informed layers, a deliberate departure from fully nonlinear recurrent networks. This creates an identifiable, control-oriented model through a tailored stochastic optimization loss, integrating Hespanha's expertise in sampling-based identification and PINN-based friction modeling into a new, principled hybrid approach.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Convex Synthesis of Robust Controllers via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Identification
proposals.motivation: Traditional controller synthesis often relies on idealized models, leading to performance degradation under real-world complexities like friction and unmodeled dynamics. Pre-2026 advances in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for system identification and stochastic optimization for control-oriented models provide a new opportunity to bridge this gap. This work aims to leverage these tools to synthesize high-performance controllers directly from data, addressing the 'design' and 'performance' gap in uncertain systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a stochastic, control-oriented identification phase with a subsequent convex optimization for controller synthesis, we can produce robust controllers that outperform those designed from standard nominal models. The key is that the identification will be tailored to preserve convexity in the subsequent synthesis step.
proposals.method: The approach has two tightly coupled stages. First, we will employ a stochastic optimization framework, akin to Hespanha's prior work, to identify a set of linear parameter-varying (LPV) or polytopic models from data, using PINN-identified friction components as known nonlinearities. Second, controller synthesis will be formulated as a convex optimization problem (e.g., Linear Matrix Inequalities) over this identified model set to guarantee robust stability and performance. The stochastic identification will explicitly sample model uncertainties to shape the convex constraints.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a systematic, end-to-end pipeline from experimental data to a certifiably robust controller, directly addressing the friction and uncertainty themes. It contributes a novel methodology that explicitly links the fidelity of data-driven identification with the feasibility of convex controller synthesis, offering practical design tools for complex mechatronic and biomedical systems.
proposals.novelty: While convex synthesis and system identification are mature fields, their integration here is novel: the identification is not just for accuracy but is *control-oriented* and *convexity-preserving*. Furthermore, incorporating PINN-identified nonlinearities (e.g., LuGre friction) as known components within a convex robust synthesis framework is a non-obvious fusion of data-driven and model-based techniques.

proposals.title: A Duality-Based Framework for Control-Oriented Estimation: From Stochastic Optimization to Physics-Informed Learning
proposals.motivation: The formal duality between optimal control and optimal estimation (e.g., Kalman filter as the dual of LQR) is a cornerstone of modern systems theory, yet its practical exploitation for complex, nonlinear, and physics-constrained problems remains limited. Recent advances in stochastic optimization and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for system identification, as seen in Hespanha's recent work, provide new computational tools to operationalize this duality for challenging domains like friction modeling and pharmacokinetics, where model uncertainty directly impacts control performance.
proposals.hypothesis: By rigorously framing the estimation problem as the dual of a suitably constructed optimal control problem, we can derive novel, computationally efficient, and physically consistent estimation algorithms that outperform conventional methods, particularly for systems with hard-to-model nonlinearities (e.g., friction) and partial observations (e.g., drug transport).
proposals.method: We will develop a two-pronged approach grounded in pre-2026 techniques. First, we will extend the stochastic optimization methods from 'Control-Oriented Identification' to solve the dual estimation problem, treating unknown parameters as control inputs to a dynamical system whose state is the estimation error. Second, we will integrate this duality framework into Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), as used in the friction modeling work, by formulating the PINN's training loss as the objective of a dual optimal control problem, thereby enforcing physical consistency and improving sample efficiency. The approach will be validated on benchmark problems and the high-precision drug transport dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a unified theoretical and computational framework that explicitly leverages control-estimation duality for system identification. It will provide new algorithms for control-oriented estimation that are more robust to noise and model mismatch. The results will advance the design of high-performance estimators for critical applications in robotics (friction) and biomedical systems (drug delivery).
proposals.novelty: While duality is well-known in theory, its systematic application to synthesize and train modern estimation architectures like PINNs via a control-theoretic lens is novel. This moves beyond using duality as an analysis tool to using it as a constructive design principle for estimation in complex, physics-constrained environments.

proposals.title: Unified, Control-Oriented System Identification via Physics-Informed Stochastic Optimization
proposals.motivation: Accurate system identification is critical for high-performance control design, especially in complex domains like friction modeling and pharmacokinetics where internal states and parameters are not directly measurable. Pre-2026, methods often trade off between physical fidelity (e.g., LuGre models) and data-driven flexibility, and struggle with the joint inference of system structure and parameters from limited, noisy input-output data.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified identification framework combining Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) with stochastic optimization can simultaneously infer both the governing structure (e.g., model order, nonlinear terms) and precise parameters of a dynamical system from input-output data, yielding models with superior predictive accuracy and control-oriented utility.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage, sampling-based algorithm. First, a PINN architecture, constrained by a library of candidate physical terms (e.g., for friction or drug transport), will be trained on the observed data to produce an initial parameter estimate and structure likelihood. Second, a control-oriented stochastic optimization routine (e.g., a modified Cross-Entropy Method) will refine these estimates by directly minimizing a multi-objective loss balancing prediction error, model parsimony, and closed-loop performance metrics predicted from the identified model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, automated pipeline for control-oriented identification that explicitly addresses the joint structure-parameter problem. It will deliver identifiable, physically plausible models directly suited for robust controller synthesis, advancing beyond pure black-box or fixed-structure methods.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a structure-selecting PINN with a performance-driven stochastic optimizer for identification is novel. It moves beyond using PINNs solely for parameter estimation in a fixed model, enabling data-driven discovery of *which* model is most appropriate for subsequent control design.

proposals.title: Robust Performance Certificates for Control Systems with Large Unmodeled Dynamics via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from robotics to biomedical devices, rely on models that inevitably neglect significant dynamics, such as complex friction or transport delays. Traditional robust control provides conservative guarantees for small, norm-bounded uncertainties but fails to offer meaningful performance bounds for large, structured unmodeled dynamics. This gap limits the safe and high-performance deployment of advanced controllers in real-world applications where model fidelity is poor.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating stochastic optimization for control-oriented identification with physics-informed neural network (PINN) models of dominant unmodeled phenomena, it is possible to derive formal, probabilistic performance guarantees for closed-loop systems that are less conservative than those from worst-case robust control.
proposals.method: The approach will first use stochastic optimization (as in Hespanha's recent work) to identify a nominal model and quantify parametric uncertainty from data. Large, structured unmodeled dynamics (e.g., friction, transport) will be represented by a transferable PINN, pre-trained on auxiliary physical data. A stochastic reachability analysis will then be performed, where the PINN's residual error and the parametric uncertainty are propagated through the closed-loop system using sampling-based methods. This will yield a distribution of possible performance metrics (e.g., L2-gain), from which probabilistic guarantees will be extracted.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for deriving non-conservative, probabilistic performance certificates for systems with significant unmodeled dynamics. It will bridge the gap between high-fidelity, learning-based modeling and formal control analysis, offering a principled design tool for performance-critical applications in robotics and precision medicine.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of physics-informed learning for unmodeled dynamics with stochastic optimization for identification and formal performance analysis, moving beyond classical small-gain or passivity theorems to provide data-informed, probabilistic guarantees.

proposals.title: Discrete-Time LaSalle Invariance Principles for Switched Linear Systems: A Lyapunov-Function-Based Framework
proposals.motivation: For continuous-time switched systems, LaSalle's invariance principle and its extensions are fundamental tools for proving asymptotic stability and convergence under arbitrary switching, especially when a common Lyapunov function is not strictly decreasing. However, a rigorous, parallel theory for discrete-time switched linear systems (SLS) is underdeveloped. This gap limits the analysis and control design for digital implementations of switched controllers and networked systems, which are inherently discrete-time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by constructing appropriate sequences of non-monotonic Lyapunov functions and leveraging the geometric properties of switched linear maps, discrete-time analogs of LaSalle's theorems can be established, providing weaker sufficient conditions for global asymptotic stability under arbitrary switching.
proposals.method: The approach will leverage control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization techniques to characterize invariant sets. We will formulate the search for a Lyapunov function and an associated auxiliary function as a semidefinite programming problem, using sum-of-squares relaxations for polynomial vector fields. For general linear subsystems, we will employ a sampling-based method to explore the state-space and construct a convergent sequence of Lyapunov-like functions, inspired by recent work on physics-informed neural networks for system identification, but applied here to verify invariance properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive discrete-time LaSalle framework for switched linear systems, enabling stability certification for a broader class of switching signals and system designs. It will yield computationally tractable stability tests and inform the design of stabilizing switched digital controllers where traditional common quadratic Lyapunov approaches are infeasible.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating the continuous-time LaSalle theory, which relies on derivatives and flow properties, into a discrete-time setting where the dynamics are governed by differences and compositions of maps, a non-trivial extension requiring new constructions for invariance and convergence.

proposals.title: Inverse Constraint Identification for Switched Systems: Inferring Switching Signal Properties from Stable Trajectories
proposals.motivation: In control of switched and hybrid systems, stability and performance guarantees depend critically on constraints on the switching signal, such as minimum dwell time or average dwell time. However, for many physical or biological systems (e.g., networked agents, pharmacokinetics), these constraints are often unknown or only partially characterized, making model-based control design challenging. An inverse approach that infers admissible switching sets from observed stable behavior would enable the analysis and control of systems where only operational data is available.
proposals.hypothesis: The set of constraints (e.g., dwell-time bounds) that define the class of admissible switching signals for a known switched linear system can be statistically identified from a finite set of observed, stable state trajectories, by formulating the problem as a stochastic feasibility optimization.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a stochastic inverse feasibility problem. Given a known switched linear system model and a dataset of state trajectories, we will parameterize the constraint set (e.g., by minimum and average dwell times). Using a sampling-based approach, we will generate candidate switching signals consistent with the observed state sequences via a particle filter or smoothed estimator. The core algorithm will perform stochastic optimization (e.g., scenario-based or likelihood-based) to find the constraint parameters that maximize the probability of generating the observed data, or that satisfy a robustness criterion over the sampled scenarios, directly extending control-oriented identification principles to the switching signal domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, data-driven methodology to identify switching constraints, a fundamental gap in hybrid system identification. It will deliver a new 'control-oriented' identification tool for switched systems, enabling robust design when switching rules are implicit. The algorithms will be validated on benchmark problems and a pharmacological compartment model, illustrating utility in systems with mode transitions, like intercompartmental drug transport.
proposals.novelty: While existing work identifies system parameters or switching sequences, the direct inference of the *constraint set governing the switching signal* from behavioral data is a novel inverse problem. The approach uniquely merges concepts from stochastic optimization, estimation for hybrid systems, and dwell-time theory in a data-driven framework.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Identification of Networked Nonlinear Systems with Coupled Stochastic Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from multi-agent robotics to networked drug delivery, operate over communication networks with inherent stochastic delays and packet losses. Pre-2026 theory often assumes these network effects are separable from plant dynamics, leading to overly optimistic performance guarantees. This work addresses the critical gap where network randomness is intrinsically coupled with a plant's internal nonlinearities, such as friction or saturations, degrading estimation and control.
proposals.hypothesis: The separability assumption between network stochasticity and plant nonlinear dynamics leads to significant identification error and suboptimal control performance. A joint identification framework that models their coupling is necessary for accurate, control-oriented models.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic optimization framework for joint identification. The method integrates a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to capture the plant's nonlinear dynamics (e.g., a LuGre friction model) with a stochastic layer modeling the network's random delays/dropouts via a Markov chain or renewal process. The coupled model parameters will be estimated using a control-oriented cost function, optimized via adaptive sampling techniques to focus on dynamically relevant regions.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a rigorous identification procedure for a critical class of networked nonlinear systems, yielding models with higher fidelity for controller design. It will deliver new stability and performance bounds for systems where network and plant dynamics are non-separable, directly impacting fields like networked robotics and precision neuromodulation.
proposals.novelty: While PINNs and network control are established separately, their integration for joint, control-oriented identification of coupled stochastic-nonlinear dynamics is novel. The approach moves beyond treating the network as an exogenous disturbance to the plant's input.

proposals.title: Inverse Stochastic Network Diagnosis: Inferring Latent Randomness from Closed-Loop Control Performance
proposals.motivation: In complex networked systems like multi-agent robotics or biological transport networks, the underlying random dynamics (e.g., packet loss, link failures, stochastic transport) are often latent and difficult to measure directly. However, their statistical characteristics critically determine achievable control performance. A method to infer these latent network statistics from readily available closed-loop performance data would enable non-invasive system diagnosis and robust controller redesign.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical parameters governing a network's random behavior (e.g., packet loss probability, delay distribution) can be uniquely inferred from the empirical distribution of a scalar, aggregate performance metric observed during the operation of a known optimal or high-performance controller.
proposals.method: We formulate this as a stochastic inverse problem. Given a known linear-quadratic-Gaussian (LQG) or model predictive control (MPC) law and observed histograms of a quadratic performance cost, we will employ a likelihood-free, simulation-based inference approach. Specifically, we will use Approximate Bayesian Computation (ABC) with sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) sampling to estimate the posterior distribution of the network's stochastic parameters. The forward model will be a control-oriented network simulator, integrating tools from Hespanha's prior work on stochastic optimization and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to regularize the search and ensure physically plausible parameter estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven framework for the non-invasive diagnosis of stochastic networks, bridging control theory and statistical inference. It will yield a practical algorithm for engineers to identify performance-degrading network characteristics without intrusive instrumentation, directly informing re-design or controller adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While system identification is mature, inverting closed-loop performance to diagnose the *network's* inherent randomness, rather than the plant dynamics, is a distinct and underexplored problem. The integration of control-oriented models with likelihood-free inference for network parameter estimation represents a novel synthesis of Hespanha's expertise.

proposals.title: Robust Online Optimization and Bandits Against an Adaptive Adversary with a Corruption Budget
proposals.motivation: Online learning and bandit algorithms are increasingly deployed in adversarial environments like network security, financial trading, and competitive control systems, where feedback signals can be strategically manipulated. Pre-2026 robust bandit literature primarily addresses stochastic corruption or oblivious adversaries, but a powerful adaptive adversary that observes the learner's state and corrupts one-bit feedback (e.g., success/failure, reward/threshold) remains a critical, under-explored threat model. This gap limits the applicability of existing algorithms in security-critical domains where feedback channels are not just noisy but malicious.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating robust statistical estimation and non-stochastic delay modeling within a control-theoretic framework, it is possible to design online learning algorithms that achieve minimax optimal regret bounds against an adaptive adversary with a known corruption budget on the one-bit feedback signal.
proposals.method: We will formulate a sequential two-player game where the adversary, constrained by a total corruption budget (e.g., C corruptions over T rounds), can flip bits, induce delays, or cause losses. The algorithmic framework will employ a robust variant of the Online Gradient Descent, using median-of-means or trimmed-mean estimators to process corrupted gradient estimates derived from one-bit feedback. For bandit settings, we will develop a robust variant of the EXP3 algorithm, incorporating a bias-corrected sampling scheme and a novel corruption-adaptive exploration schedule. The adversary's actions will be modeled as a non-stochastic disturbance, analyzed using tools from Hespanha's work on control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first minimax optimal regret guarantees for online learning and bandits with adaptively corrupted one-bit feedback, explicitly bridging robust statistics and adversarial learning theory. It will deliver a principled, implementable algorithmic framework with provable security guarantees for applications like resilient sensor networks and robust controller tuning where feedback is vulnerable to manipulation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on stochastic corruption or oblivious adversaries, this proposal directly addresses an adaptive, state-aware adversary within the one-bit feedback constraint, combining robust gradient estimation with a control-theoretic analysis of strategic corruption delays—a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Analytical Optimization of Step-Size Distributions and Feedback Gains for Control-Oriented Stochastic Identification
proposals.motivation: Stochastic optimization algorithms, such as those used in control-oriented system identification, rely heavily on heuristic choices for step-sizes and sampling distributions, which critically affect convergence rate and final estimation performance. In complex, multi-scale systems like drug transport or friction dynamics, suboptimal tuning leads to excessive data requirements, poor real-time control adaptability, and unreliable parameter estimates. An analytical framework to derive these parameters from the problem structure itself is needed to move beyond trial-and-error.
proposals.hypothesis: The analytical model of the identification error dynamics, combined with a known or estimated parameter distribution, can be used to derive provably optimal step-size sequences and feedback correction strategies that minimize a finite-horizon performance criterion.
proposals.method: We will formulate the parameter estimation process as a stochastic optimal control problem where the state is the parameter estimation error. Using the analytical gradient and Hessian information from Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) models or linearized dynamics, we will apply dynamic programming and stochastic approximation theory to derive closed-form expressions for the optimal step-size as a function of the iteration and current error covariance. Feedback strategies will be designed by treating measurement updates as a control input to minimize a quadratic cost of error and computational effort.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, theoretically grounded methodology for tuning stochastic identification algorithms, directly linking problem geometry to algorithmic hyperparameters. It will yield concrete design rules for step-size schedules and adaptive feedback in applications like LuGre friction identification and in-situ pharmacokinetic modeling, improving convergence guarantees and data efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive step-size heuristics (e.g., Adam) are common, deriving them as the solution to a formal optimal control problem defined by the specific identification task's analytical model is a novel synthesis of control theory and stochastic optimization. This moves beyond generic rules to problem-specific optimal tuning.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization for Sample-Efficient Phase Control in High-Precision Dynamical Systems
proposals.motivation: Optimal phase control in systems like quantum circuits or precision mechatronics is critical for performance but often relies on expensive, data-intensive search. Pre-2026, model-free evolutionary algorithms are inefficient, wasting experimental or high-fidelity simulation trials. A control-oriented, sample-efficient method is needed to optimize performance metrics like fidelity or yield where each evaluation is costly.
proposals.hypothesis: A surrogate model-based optimization loop, using Gaussian Process regression to learn the phase-perturbation-to-performance map, will converge to optimal phase configurations with significantly fewer evaluations than blind search by intelligently exploiting landscape structure and uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework using Stochastic Optimization to fit a Physics-Informed Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model from sparse, noisy evaluations. An acquisition function (e.g., Expected Improvement) will leverage the GP's predictive mean and variance to analytically select the most informative phase perturbation for the next trial. This loop integrates directly with experimental or high-fidelity simulation setups, iteratively refining the model and control policy towards the optimum.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, sample-efficient algorithm for high-precision phase control, directly applicable to problems in quantum control and friction compensation. It will extend control-oriented identification to Bayesian optimization settings and demonstrate robust convergence on benchmark systems with quantifiable data efficiency gains.
proposals.novelty: The integration of Physics-Informed Neural Networks or kernel structures within the GP surrogate to encode prior dynamical knowledge (e.g., from LuGre-type friction models) for faster learning, combined with a control-theoretic formulation of the acquisition policy, moves beyond generic Bayesian optimization.

proposals.title: Analytically-Guided Thompson Sampling for Adaptive Distributed Optimization in Networked Systems
proposals.motivation: Distributed optimization algorithms, such as those for sensor network coordination or multi-agent control, rely on critical parameters (e.g., step-sizes, gains) that significantly impact convergence and performance. These parameters are typically set statically, but optimal values depend on dynamic, a priori unknown network conditions like connectivity and latency. Pure model-free learning (e.g., bandits) is data-inefficient, while pure analytical models cannot adapt to unmodeled dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: That a Bayesian multi-armed bandit framework, whose action space, reward structure, and prior distributions are explicitly shaped by an analytical control-oriented model, will achieve provably faster adaptation and superior regret bounds in distributed optimization tasks compared to standard bandit or static model-based approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer architecture. First, an analytical layer uses a simplified, identifiable model of the networked system (e.g., a consensus protocol with friction) to formulate the parameter tuning problem as a stochastic optimization, deriving optimal parameter sensitivities and feasible regions. This shapes the bandit's action set and provides an informative prior for a Thompson Sampling agent. The bandit layer then selects parameters in real-time, receiving a noisy, global performance reward. The analytical model is subsequently refined via the bandit's data using pre-2026 control-oriented identification techniques, closing the loop.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, hybrid framework for adaptive parameter selection in distributed control that rigorously fuses model-based optimality with data-driven learning. 2) New theoretical regret analysis for analytically-guided bandits, demonstrating accelerated convergence. 3) Validation in high-fidelity simulations of networked systems with time-varying communication constraints.
proposals.novelty: The systematic use of an analytical control model not just to define the problem, but to *derive the structured prior and action space* for a Bayesian bandit, creating a tight, bi-directional coupling rather than a simple sequential or parallel combination of methods.

proposals.title: Non-Asymptotic Stochastic Approximation for Heterogeneous and Asynchronous Multi-Agent Systems: A Control-Theoretic Framework
proposals.motivation: Federated and decentralized learning algorithms face practical non-idealities—statistical heterogeneity, asynchronous updates, and delayed communications—that degrade performance and complicate tuning. Classical stochastic approximation (SA) theory, relying on homogeneous and asymptotic assumptions, fails to provide actionable guidelines for these real-world settings, leading to heuristic and inefficient deployments.
proposals.hypothesis: A refined SA framework that explicitly models agent-specific data distributions, adaptive local stepsizes, and intermittent communications can yield non-asymptotic convergence bounds with an explicit error decomposition, enabling the principled design of adaptive tuning rules that balance optimization error with the costs of system heterogeneity.
proposals.method: We will develop a heterogeneous-agent SA model where each agent performs local stochastic gradient steps with its own stepsize sequence, communicating sporadically with delays. Using martingale and Lyapunov analysis techniques from non-asymptotic control theory, we will derive finite-time convergence rates. The analysis will separate error terms for optimization, data heterogeneity, and communication asynchrony. This decomposition will then inform the design of control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization rules for adaptively tuning local stepsizes and communication frequencies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for distributed SA under realistic heterogeneous and asynchronous conditions. It will yield a set of verifiable conditions and adaptive tuning protocols that translate theoretical insights into practical algorithm configuration guidelines for federated learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The explicit, non-asymptotic error decomposition linking practical non-idealities to concrete performance metrics is novel, moving beyond homogeneous or asymptotic SA theory. Integrating this analysis with control-oriented tuning rules creates a closed-loop design perspective absent from prior work.

proposals.title: Mean-Field Stochastic Approximation: A Statistical Physics Framework for Convergence of Distributed Learning
proposals.motivation: Distributed stochastic approximation is fundamental for large-scale learning and control in sensor networks and multi-agent systems. However, rigorous convergence analysis for networks of interacting agents remains intractable due to complex, coupled stochastic dynamics. This gap limits the reliable design of algorithms for applications like distributed estimation or federated learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The aggregate interaction in a distributed stochastic approximation process can be modeled as a deterministic mean-field, allowing the algorithm's trajectory to be associated with a tractable limiting ordinary differential equation (ODE). Convergence can then be proven by establishing the ODE's stability and demonstrating that the stochastic process tracks its solution.
proposals.method: We will adapt mean-field theory from statistical physics by decomposing the local update rule into a local stochastic approximation term and a deterministic term representing the network's average influence. Using tools from stochastic approximation theory and martingale analysis, we will formally derive the associated mean-field ODE. The core technical steps involve proving the ODE's global asymptotic stability under specific network connectivity and data assumptions, and then establishing a probabilistic bound on the deviation between the stochastic process and the ODE solution using a martingale concentration argument.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, general analytical framework for proving convergence in distributed stochastic approximation, delivering explicit convergence rates and robustness guarantees. It will yield concrete design guidelines for network topology and step-size schedules to ensure stability, directly impacting the design of reliable distributed estimation and control algorithms.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of statistical physics' mean-field methodology to the analysis of distributed stochastic algorithms provides a principled path to tractability where direct analysis fails. This cross-disciplinary analogy moves beyond standard Lyapunov or consensus-based analyses common in control theory pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Sliding Mode Framework for Robust Distributed Optimization with Noisy Gradients and Delays
proposals.motivation: Distributed learning in multi-agent systems is critical for large-scale data processing and cooperative control, but suffers from robustness issues due to noisy local gradients, communication delays, and adversarial data points. Pre-2026 methods, such as decentralized stochastic gradient descent, lack formal guarantees of finite-time convergence and stability under such persistent disturbances, limiting their reliability in safety-critical applications like networked robotics or distributed sensor fusion.
proposals.hypothesis: By synthesizing stochastic approximation theory with sliding mode control, we can design a novel class of distributed learning algorithms that provably achieve finite-time convergence to a bounded region despite persistent disturbances, a property neither approach achieves alone in distributed settings.
proposals.method: We will formulate the sliding surface dynamics as a stochastic approximation process, embedding the consensus and innovation terms of distributed optimization. A discontinuous control term, designed via a Lyapunov-based approach, will enforce sliding motion. We will analyze the resulting stochastic differential inclusion using martingale and averaging theory to derive convergence rates and stability bounds. The algorithm will be validated on a distributed estimation problem for a multi-robot system with simulated packet loss and gradient noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical framework with formal robustness guarantees for distributed learning, bridging control-theoretic stability analysis with statistical learning theory. It will yield a practical, provably robust algorithm for cooperative multi-agent systems operating under realistic communication and data constraints.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in designing the sliding surface itself as a stochastic approximation process, enabling the direct application of sliding mode's finite-time convergence properties to the stochastic, distributed optimization problem, which prior work has not addressed.

proposals.title: Convergence Analysis of Distributed, Control-Oriented System Identification via Stochastic Approximation Theory
proposals.motivation: Distributed, data-driven system identification is critical for large-scale networked systems, such as multi-agent robotic teams or sensor networks. However, existing algorithms often lack formal convergence guarantees, especially when using stochastic optimization for control-oriented metrics. This gap limits their reliability in safety-critical applications where performance and stability assurances are required.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distributed, gradient-based identification algorithms, such as those derived from Hespanha's recent work on control-oriented identification, can be rigorously modeled as distributed stochastic approximation (SA) processes, enabling formal proofs of almost-sure convergence to a consensus parameter estimate under realistic noise and communication conditions.
proposals.method: We will model the distributed identification algorithm as a multi-agent stochastic approximation process with consensus constraints. Using the ODE method from SA theory, we will analyze the associated limiting differential inclusion. We will employ specific techniques from the pre-2026 literature on distributed SA (e.g., consensus+innovation frameworks) to handle non-i.i.d. data streams arising from closed-loop operation. The analysis will explicitly account for the sampling-based gradient approximations central to the 'Control-Oriented Identification via Stochastic Optimization' approach.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal convergence proof for a class of distributed, control-oriented identification algorithms, bridging a gap between practical stochastic optimization and theoretical SA. It will yield explicit, verifiable conditions on step-sizes, network connectivity, and excitation signals required for convergence, directly informing the design of reliable networked identification schemes.
proposals.novelty: While SA theory is mature, its application to prove convergence of *distributed* identification algorithms optimized for *control performance* (rather than prediction error) is novel. The analysis must handle the complex coupling between the distributed SA dynamics and the control-oriented cost function, which is non-standard in classical identification.

proposals.title: Minimal Feedback Rate for Target SNR: A Stochastic Optimization and Control-Oriented Identification Framework
proposals.motivation: In networked control systems and remote estimation, communication bandwidth is a critical resource. Classical results like the data-rate theorem provide asymptotic bounds, but a practical, design-oriented theory for the minimal feedback rate required to achieve a specific, finite Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) performance target is lacking. This problem is central to the efficient design of systems with constrained communication, such as multi-agent robotic teams or remote sensor networks.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given linear time-invariant plant with stochastic disturbances and a target output SNR, there exists a fundamental lower bound on the required feedback data rate, and this bound can be approached via a co-design of a parsimonious encoder/estimator and an optimal controller.
proposals.method: We will formulate the problem as a constrained stochastic optimization, minimizing the mutual information (as a rate proxy) between the encoder output and the plant state subject to a hard constraint on the closed-loop output variance (defining the SNR). The approach leverages control-oriented identification techniques to build low-order models capturing essential dynamics. We will employ a nested optimization loop: an outer loop using stochastic approximation (e.g., simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation) to tune encoder quantization parameters, and an inner loop solving a standard LQG problem for a given encoder to evaluate the SNR constraint.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, computationally tractable methodology for jointly designing minimal-feedback communication schemes and controllers to meet precise performance specifications. It will yield explicit trade-off curves (rate vs. SNR) for benchmark problems, offering practical guidelines for system architects.
proposals.novelty: Unlike asymptotic data-rate theorems, this work provides a finite-precision, design-centric solution for a specific SNR target. It novelly combines rate-distortion ideas with stochastic optimal control and control-oriented identification in a single optimization framework.

proposals.title: Adaptive Control of Networked Systems with Unmodeled Communication Delays via Online Stochastic Identification and Robust Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Networked control systems (NCS) underpin critical infrastructure and autonomous systems, but their performance degrades under uncertain, time-varying communication delays. Pre-2026 control theory largely relies on a known, bounded delay assumption, which is often violated in real-world networks (e.g., wireless, shared channels), leading to instability or poor performance. Removing this restrictive assumption is essential for deploying robust, high-performance control in unpredictable communication environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling a real-time, stochastic optimization framework for delay identification with a robust, adaptive control synthesis, we can stabilize and guarantee performance for NCS without prior knowledge of delay bounds. The key is that the delay's statistical properties, learned online, provide sufficient information for control re-design.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer architecture. First, a control-oriented identification layer will use stochastic optimization (e.g., particle filtering or stochastic gradient) to estimate the time-varying delay's parameters and statistics from input-output data, inspired by Hespanha's prior work on identification. Second, a robust controller synthesis layer will adaptively tune a parameterized controller (e.g., using a Youla-Kučera parameterization or a predictor-based structure) based on the current delay model. The adaptation will be formulated as a sampled-data optimal control problem, solved periodically via convex optimization or gradient-based methods, ensuring stability through Lyapunov redesign or small-gain arguments.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, practical framework for NCS control that does not require a priori delay bounds, significantly expanding applicability. It will provide formal stability and performance guarantees under online learning, bridging the gap between adaptive identification and robust control. The resulting algorithms will be validated in simulation for scenarios like vehicle platooning and remote robotics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, real-time integration of stochastic delay identification directly into a robust control synthesis loop, moving beyond passive robustness to active, model-informed adaptation. This approach is distinct from prior methods that either assume known bounds or use overly conservative fixed controllers.

proposals.title: Quantifying Transient Performance in Hybrid Control: A Stochastic Optimization Framework for Convergence Rate Estimation
proposals.motivation: Hybrid control systems, which combine continuous and discrete dynamics, are central to modern robotics and cyber-physical systems. While stability analysis is mature, pre-2026 design often lacks rigorous, quantitative metrics for transient performance, such as convergence rates and overshoot during mode transitions. This gap limits the ability to predict and optimize real-world system behavior, where performance during transients is as critical as asymptotic stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transient performance of a hybrid control scheme can be rigorously quantified by formulating the convergence rate as a stochastic optimization problem over system trajectories, and that this quantification will reveal fundamental design trade-offs obscured by classical stability certificates.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework using stochastic optimization, specifically tailored for hybrid systems. The method involves sampling system trajectories under the hybrid controller and using a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture to learn a Lyapunov-like function that explicitly encodes convergence rate information. This function's parameters will be optimized via a stochastic gradient algorithm to minimize a cost functional that penalizes slow convergence and large transients, directly linking identification to performance quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, optimization-based methodology for quantifying transient performance in hybrid control, moving beyond binary stability assessments. It will yield new, computable metrics for convergence rates and offer concrete design guidelines for improving transient response. The integration of PINNs for function approximation within a stochastic optimization loop represents a novel synthesis of data-driven and model-based techniques for performance analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing transient performance quantification explicitly as a stochastic optimization problem over system executions, rather than as an afterthought to stability proofs. Furthermore, leveraging PINNs to learn performance-certifying functions for hybrid systems is a non-obvious extension of their pre-2026 use in friction identification and system modeling.

proposals.title: Optimal State Estimation under Joint Constraints of Limited Sampling Rate and Network-Induced Delays: A Stochastic Optimization and Dual Problem Framework
proposals.motivation: Modern networked control systems, such as remote surgical robotics or distributed sensor networks, rely on state estimation from sensors with inherent limitations. These include a maximum sampling rate (limited data rate) and unpredictable network delays, which degrade estimation performance and compromise closed-loop stability. Existing estimation theory often treats these constraints separately, lacking a unified framework that explicitly addresses their combined, non-classical information pattern for optimal design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimal estimator for a linear system with limited-rate, delayed measurements can be formulated and solved as a stochastic optimization problem over a dual space, where the constraints on information availability define a novel, tractable structure for the estimator gain.
proposals.method: We will formulate the estimation error covariance minimization as a constrained stochastic optimization problem, where the decision variable is the causal estimator mapping. The limited-rate and delayed data constraint will be encoded as an information constraint in the optimization. We will employ a control-oriented identification approach, using stochastic approximation (e.g., simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation - SPSA) to numerically solve for the optimal estimator gains in the dual problem space. The framework will be validated on a benchmark problem involving the estimation of brain drug concentration from delayed, sparsely sampled venous measurements, extending prior pharmacokinetic models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, unified methodology for designing optimal estimators under realistic networked sensing constraints, directly linking estimation performance bounds to physical sensor and network parameters. It will deliver a computationally tractable algorithm for estimator synthesis, advancing the field of networked estimation and control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit joint treatment of limited sampling rate and delay within a single stochastic optimization framework, and the exploration of the dual problem structure for estimation, which is less studied than its counterpart in optimal control.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Model Reduction and Event-Triggered Hybrid Control for Low-Power Embedded Systems
proposals.motivation: Hybrid controllers, which combine continuous dynamics with discrete logic, are essential for complex embedded systems like medical devices and robotics. However, their computational complexity and power consumption often preclude implementation on resource-constrained hardware. Pre-2026, there is a critical need to bridge the gap between sophisticated control design and practical, low-power embedded execution.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing a simplified, control-oriented system identification process with an event-triggered hybrid control law, we can drastically reduce online computational load while preserving closed-loop performance guarantees for systems with friction and switching dynamics.
proposals.method: We will leverage Prof. Hespanha's expertise in stochastic optimization and physics-informed models. First, we will develop a control-oriented identification method using stochastic optimization to fit parsimonious, piecewise-affine models with embedded LuGre friction dynamics, minimizing the model's state dimension. Second, we will synthesize a hybrid controller using optimal sampling-based techniques to pre-compute a library of robust controllers for each affine region. Online implementation will use an event-triggered scheduler, executing control updates only when necessary, drastically reducing CPU cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a systematic framework for designing computationally frugal hybrid controllers, validated on a low-power embedded platform. It will contribute a novel methodology for joint model reduction and control synthesis, explicitly targeting implementation complexity as a primary design constraint.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of parsimonious, physics-informed identification for hybrid systems with the synthesis of event-triggered control laws, moving beyond standard model reduction which often ignores control performance, and beyond standard event-triggering designed for full-complexity models.

proposals.title: Optimal Dynamic Output Feedback for Networked Control Systems with Uncertain Friction and Delays
proposals.motivation: Networked control systems (NCS) for robotics and autonomous agents often rely on static state feedback, assuming perfect, instantaneous state knowledge. This is unrealistic under network delays, packet loss, and partial observations, especially when dynamics involve hard-to-measure nonlinearities like friction. A dynamic output feedback framework is essential for robust performance but poses significant design challenges in distributed settings.
proposals.hypothesis: A co-design methodology integrating a dynamic output feedback controller with a model-based state estimator, explicitly accounting for network imperfections and identified friction dynamics, will yield superior closed-loop performance and robustness compared to static feedback laws under the same constraints.
proposals.method: We will formulate a stochastic optimal control problem where the controller has access only to delayed, noisy output measurements over a network. The method combines: 1) A Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to identify a LuGre-type friction model from experimental data, ensuring physically plausible dynamics. 2) A Kalman-type filter, designed via Control-Oriented Identification techniques, to estimate the full state (including internal friction states) from the outputs. 3) A dynamic controller, synthesized via gradient-based stochastic optimization (e.g., policy gradient or backpropagation through time), that processes the estimator's output to generate optimal control signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, control-oriented framework for designing dynamic output feedback laws for NCS with nonlinear physical interactions. It will deliver concrete algorithms for controller-estimator co-design and demonstrate quantifiable performance gains in applications like networked robotic manipulators or autonomous vehicle platoons subject to friction and communication constraints.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a physics-constrained friction identifier (PINN) within a stochastic optimal dynamic output feedback design loop for NCS moves beyond the standard separation principle, which often fails under network non-idealities and model uncertainty, offering a truly joint optimization of estimation and control.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Online Estimation and Adaptation for Systems with Slowly Time-Varying Dynamics
proposals.motivation: High-performance control in domains like precision mechatronics and biomedical systems (e.g., drug delivery) often assumes Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) models. However, real-world systems exhibit parameter drift due to wear (friction changes), environmental shifts, or physiological adaptation, degrading the performance of controllers designed for fixed dynamics.
