Existing robust control methods can be conservative, while adaptive control theories often lack a systematic, data-driven identification framework suitable for control re-design.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual estimation framework that jointly identifies a slowly time-varying LuGre-type friction model (or analogous process dynamics) and updates a control-oriented model online will enable the synthesis of adaptive controllers that maintain optimal performance despite parameter drift.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic optimization algorithm for recursive, online identification. The core is a two-timescale approach: a fast inner loop uses Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for high-precision, seconds-resolved estimation of time-varying parameters from streaming data. A slower outer loop employs this updated model in a Model Predictive Control (MPC) or LQG synthesis problem, solved via efficient sampling-based stochastic optimization to compute a new optimal control law. The architecture ensures stability by constraining parameter variations within pre-learned bounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled methodology for merging high-fidelity, online system identification with optimal control redesign for time-varying systems. It will yield concrete algorithms for adaptive control in friction-dominated systems and biomedical applications, demonstrated via simulation and experimental case studies. The framework extends 'control-oriented identification' to a dynamic, time-varying setting.
proposals.novelty: The integration of PINNs for real-time, physics-constrained tracking of time-varying parameters directly into a stochastic optimization loop for control synthesis is novel. It moves beyond batch identification or robust static control, offering a proactive adaptation paradigm grounded in Hespanha's prior work on friction identification and control-oriented methods.

proposals.title: Stochastic Delay Modeling and Control-Oriented Identification for Networked Systems
proposals.motivation: In networked control systems and biological networks, time delays are typically modeled as bounded uncertainties, leading to overly conservative control designs that sacrifice performance. Pre-2026, there is a recognized need to leverage probabilistic delay information, often available from network traffic analysis or pharmacokinetic studies, to design less conservative, higher-performance controllers.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing bounded uncertain delay models with identified stochastic delay distributions will enable the synthesis of controllers that are less conservative and achieve superior performance while maintaining robust stability guarantees.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework using stochastic optimization to fit parametric probability distributions (e.g., Gamma, Lognormal) to delay measurements from a target system. This identified stochastic model will then inform the synthesis of an optimal controller via a sampled-data or probabilistic robust control formulation, potentially using a scenario-based approach or a tailored Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI) framework that incorporates the delay distribution's moments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic methodology to transition from bounded-delay to stochastic-delay control design, yielding a new class of performance-enhanced controllers. It will also deliver a novel identification tool tailored for control synthesis, bridging the gap between statistical modeling and robust control theory.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic delays have been studied in queuing theory, their integration into a principled, control-oriented identification and synthesis loop is novel. This moves beyond treating delays as purely adversarial (bounded) or purely random (simple averages) to a nuanced, distributionally robust perspective.

proposals.title: Stability and Performance Analysis of Nonlinear Networked Control Systems via an Impulsive Hybrid Systems Framework
proposals.motivation: Networked Control Systems (NCSs) are ubiquitous but suffer from network-induced imperfections like delays and packet dropouts. While the impulsive system framework has successfully modeled these effects for linear plants, most real-world systems are nonlinear. A general framework for nonlinear NCSs is needed to provide rigorous stability guarantees and performance bounds for applications like multi-robot systems and smart grids.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stability and performance of NCSs with nonlinear plant/controller dynamics, subject to stochastic network events, can be rigorously analyzed by embedding the system into a nonlinear impulsive hybrid model and deriving sufficient stability conditions using a stochastic Lyapunov approach.
proposals.method: We will model the nonlinear NCS as a stochastic hybrid impulsive system, where the continuous flow describes nonlinear dynamics and the jumps model network updates. The core technical approach involves constructing a control-oriented identification step using Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to learn unknown nonlinear friction-like terms from data. Subsequently, we will formulate a stochastic optimization problem to co-design a sampled-data controller and network sampling policy, leveraging tools from optimal impulsive control and Lyapunov-based stability analysis for nonlinear hybrid systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the established impulsive systems theory for NCSs to the nonlinear domain, providing a new set of sufficient conditions for stochastic stability. It will deliver a co-design methodology for nonlinear controllers and event-triggered sampling schemes that explicitly account for network constraints, advancing the practical design of high-performance NCSs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of data-driven nonlinear identification (via PINNs) with formal stochastic hybrid systems analysis for NCSs, moving beyond the linear or simple nonlinear cases typically addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Robust Control Synthesis for Networked Control Systems with Norm-Bounded Uncertainties in Sampling and Delay
proposals.motivation: Networked Control Systems (NCS) are fundamental to modern cyber-physical systems but suffer from performance degradation and instability due to time-varying delays and packet loss. Pre-2026 robust control methods often treat these network imperfections as stochastic or use overly conservative bounds, limiting their applicability to high-performance domains like teleoperation or multi-agent systems where predictable robustness is critical.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the combined effects of uncertain, variable sampling intervals and network-induced delays as a structured, norm-bounded uncertainty on a nominal discrete-time model, one can synthesize a robust dynamic output-feedback controller that guarantees stability and a specified H-infinity performance level.
proposals.method: The approach will first derive an uncertain discrete-time system model where the uncertain sampling period and delay are captured via a linear fractional transformation (LFT) with norm-bounded blocks. We will then apply control-oriented identification via stochastic optimization (as in Hespanha's recent work) to characterize the uncertainty bounds from experimental data. Finally, the controller will be synthesized by solving a convex optimization problem formulated using the scaled small-gain theorem and linear matrix inequalities (LMIs) to minimize the closed-loop induced L2-gain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, computationally tractable framework for robust controller design in NCS with explicit, non-conservative performance guarantees. It will deliver a set of verifiable LMI conditions for stability and a systematic procedure to translate network imperfection data into a control-oriented uncertainty model.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integrated LFT modeling of coupled sampling and delay uncertainties for controller synthesis, combined with a data-driven identification step specifically tailored for robust control design, moving beyond common stochastic or deterministic worst-case approaches.

proposals.title: Optimal Event-Triggered Sampling for Performance-Driven Control: A Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Modern networked and resource-constrained control systems, such as robotic manipulators or biomedical delivery devices, require efficient sampling to manage communication and computation. Traditional event-triggered control focuses primarily on stability guarantees, often using conservative thresholds that lead to suboptimal performance in terms of tracking error, energy use, or wear (e.g., friction-induced). There is a critical need to co-design the sampling law and the control update to directly optimize a system-level performance metric, not just ensure stability.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the joint design of the sampling law and control policy as a stochastic optimization problem over a performance metric, we can derive adaptive, performance-driven sampling strategies that significantly outperform conventional stability-triggered methods.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as a constrained stochastic optimization, where the objective is a composite performance metric (e.g., L2 gain plus a communication cost) and the constraints enforce closed-loop stability. The core algorithm will involve a two-level structure: (1) an outer loop using a derivative-free stochastic optimization method (e.g., Bayesian optimization or a cross-entropy method) to explore the space of parameterized sampling laws (e.g., threshold functions); and (2) an inner loop employing control-oriented identification via simulation or a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) to model the system's response (including friction dynamics) and evaluate the performance metric for a given law. The update law will be simultaneously optimized using techniques from optimal control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic methodology for designing performance-optimal event-triggered sampling laws, moving beyond stability-centric triggers. It will deliver concrete, tunable algorithms for resource-constrained control systems and demonstrate quantified performance gains in applications like precision motion control (with friction) and networked drug delivery models.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the inverse problem formulation: directly optimizing the sampling law for a holistic performance metric, rather than deriving it as a byproduct of a Lyapunov stability proof. This integrates stochastic optimization for sampling design with physics-informed models in a control-oriented framework, which is a distinct departure from existing literature.

proposals.title: Optimal Observer Design for Single-Rate Systems: Revisiting the Necessity of Blocking Mechanisms
proposals.motivation: In networked and embedded control systems, multi-rate measurement scenarios, where sensor updates are slower than the controller's actuation rate, are common. A standard solution is to employ a blocking mechanism, which holds the last received measurement for multiple control steps. However, it remains unclear if this heuristic is theoretically justified or optimal for systems where the sensor and actuator rates are eventually matched (single-rate), as it may introduce unnecessary latency and degrade performance. This investigation is crucial for designing high-performance, low-latency control systems in robotics and process control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for single-rate systems, the standard blocking mechanism is suboptimal and that a redesigned observer, which directly fuses all available historical measurement data without artificial blocking, can yield superior estimation and closed-loop performance.
proposals.method: We will formulate a control-oriented identification and observer design problem within a stochastic optimization framework. The core technical approach involves developing a novel Kalman-filter-based observer that operates at the fast control rate but is informed by an irregular, time-stamped measurement history, treating delayed or infrequent measurements as constraints rather than applying a blocking hold. We will leverage Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to learn latent system dynamics and noise characteristics, facilitating the observer design. Performance will be evaluated against standard blocking methods using metrics of estimation error and closed-loop control cost in simulated robotic systems with friction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous analysis and a new design framework for observers in single-rate systems, challenging the conventional use of blocking. It will deliver concrete performance bounds and design guidelines, potentially leading to lower-latency, higher-precision control algorithms applicable to fields like precision robotics and drug delivery systems where model fidelity is critical.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically questioning a foundational assumption (blocking) in multi-rate control and proposing a tailored, optimal estimation strategy for the single-rate case, which is often the final implementation target. Combining stochastic optimization for identification with a history-aware observer design specifically for this scenario is unexplored.

proposals.title: Quantifying Robustness Margins for Control-Oriented Models via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Sampling
proposals.motivation: Controllers designed using models identified from limited data, as in drug transport or friction systems, can fail catastrophically under large plant-model mismatches. Pre-2026, methods like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) improve model fidelity, but a systematic framework to analyze the performance limits and robustness guarantees of such controllers against significant, structured uncertainties is lacking. This gap is critical for safety-critical applications like neural drug delivery or precision motion control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formulating robustness analysis as a stochastic optimization problem over a constrained uncertainty set, we can compute probabilistic performance bounds and identify the specific model mismatch structures (e.g., in friction parameters or transport rates) that most degrade closed-loop performance.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage, control-oriented identification framework. First, we will use PINN-derived friction or pharmacokinetic models as nominal plants. Second, we will define a high-dimensional uncertainty set parameterizing large mismatches in physical parameters and unmodeled dynamics. Using a stochastic optimization approach—specifically, a tailored variant of the Cross-Entropy Method for rare-event sampling—we will solve a constrained optimization problem to find the worst-case uncertainty realization that violates a given performance metric. This will be integrated with a sensitivity analysis to pinpoint critical uncertainty directions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, sampling-based methodology to quantify robustness margins for controllers derived from data-driven, physics-informed models. It will yield actionable insights into which model inaccuracies require priority correction during the identification phase. The results will advance the design of high-performance, uncertainty-aware control systems for complex, partially known plants.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging control-oriented identification via stochastic optimization with a physics-informed uncertainty sampling strategy to explicitly analyze large-mismatch robustness, moving beyond traditional small-gain or linear robustness analysis. This creates a direct link between the fidelity of learned physical models and verifiable closed-loop control guarantees.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented Data-Driven Identification of Blocking Dynamics via Physics-Informed Stochastic Optimization
proposals.motivation: In control systems, 'blocking' refers to a system's ability to reject or attenuate specific disturbances or signals. Traditional model-based design of blocking controllers relies on accurate first-principles models, which are often unavailable for complex systems like biological drug transport or systems with intricate friction. Pre-2026 advances in data-driven system identification and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a path to learn these dynamics directly from input-output data, enabling robust control design where models are uncertain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a control-oriented, data-driven identification framework, which explicitly encodes the structural prior of signal blocking into the learning objective, can yield dynamical models that are significantly more effective for subsequent optimal controller synthesis than models identified for pure prediction accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop a stochastic optimization framework where the identification cost function penalizes prediction error and incorporates a control-oriented penalty term reflecting the closed-loop blocking performance specification. The system dynamics will be parameterized using neural network architectures, specifically Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), to embed known physical constraints (e.g., dissipation, mass balance) and improve data efficiency. The optimization will be performed using gradient-based methods suitable for the non-convex landscape, drawing on Hespanha's prior work in control-oriented identification and sampling-based stochastic optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a systematic methodology for learning dynamical models tailored explicitly for blocking control tasks. It will provide concrete algorithms and validation on benchmark problems, potentially in friction compensation and pharmaco-kinetic systems, demonstrating improved controller performance over using generically identified models. This bridges the gap between high-fidelity data-driven modeling and robust control design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of the closed-loop control objective (blocking) directly into the system identification phase via a tailored cost function, moving beyond the standard two-step process of first identifying a model and then designing a controller. This co-design approach for identification and control is non-trivial and leverages modern PINN architectures for physically consistent learning.

proposals.title: Optimal Output Regulation with Guaranteed Minimum Control Effort via a Stochastic Primal-Dual Framework
proposals.motivation: The classical output regulation problem ensures asymptotic tracking and disturbance rejection but often yields controllers with excessive control action, wasting energy and causing undue actuator wear. In applications like precision mechatronics or biomedical drug delivery, where control authority is physically or physiologically constrained, minimizing control effort is paramount for performance and safety. Pre-2026 methods treat regulation and energy minimization sequentially or heuristically, lacking a unified, control-oriented framework with formal guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating output regulation as a constrained stochastic optimization problem—where regulation is a hard constraint and control energy is the objective—a primal-dual solution will yield a feedback law that achieves exact regulation with provably minimal expected control effort for a given class of disturbances.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method grounded in Hespanha's recent work on control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization. First, a physics-informed neural network (PINN) or sampling-based method will identify a stochastic, control-oriented model of the plant and exosystem (disturbances/references). Second, we will solve the resulting constrained stochastic optimal control problem via a tailored primal-dual algorithm. The primal step updates the controller parameters to minimize a quadratic control energy metric, while the dual step enforces the regulation condition (steady-state error dynamics) via Lagrange multipliers, using stochastic gradient estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical and computational framework for energy-aware regulation, delivering explicit bounds on minimal achievable effort. It will yield a practical design tool for resource-constrained systems, demonstrated in a case study such as friction-compensated motion control or minimally invasive brain drug transport regulation, where precise tracking must be achieved with minimal intervention.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the strict dual problem formulation—treating regulation as a constraint rather than a cost term—and its solution via a stochastic primal-dual algorithm derived from control-oriented identification, moving beyond classical LQR or H2 regulation synthesis. This directly addresses the trade-off at the constraint boundary, which is non-obvious in standard weighted cost designs.

proposals.title: Event-Triggered Stochastic Optimal Control for Multi-Rate Systems with Friction and Transport Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Multi-rate periodic sampling is standard in networked control systems (e.g., for drug transport or mechanical systems with friction) but is often inefficient, leading to unnecessary communication and computation when system states are near equilibrium. Event-triggered control, which samples and actuates only when needed, promises significant resource savings. However, its integration with stochastic optimal control frameworks for complex, nonlinear dynamics like those in Hespanha's recent work on friction and drug transport remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: By synthesizing event-triggered sampling rules with a stochastic optimal control architecture, we can achieve performance comparable to traditional multi-rate control while drastically reducing the average sampling rate and computational load for systems with LuGre friction or intercompartmental transport dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework using Stochastic Optimization to create parsimonious, event-triggered models of the plant (e.g., a LuGre friction system or a drug transport model). The core controller will be designed using an optimal stochastic control law, but its execution will be governed by a triggering condition based on the prediction error of a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) state estimator. When the error exceeds a designed threshold, a new sample is taken, and the control input is recomputed. We will rigorously analyze the trade-off between sampling rate and control performance (e.g., L2-gain).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled design methodology for event-triggered control in nonlinear, stochastic systems, directly applicable to high-precision mechanical and biomedical control problems. It will yield concrete performance guarantees and a significant reduction in resource usage compared to fixed multi-rate schemes, advancing the practical implementation of resource-aware control systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a data-driven, physics-informed event trigger with a stochastic optimal control law, specifically tailored for the complex dynamics (friction, transport) studied in Hespanha's group, moving beyond the linear systems typically addressed in event-triggered control literature.

proposals.title: Optimal Control and Estimation Under Adversarial Actuator and System Dynamics Corruption
proposals.motivation: Modern cyber-physical systems, from autonomous vehicles to medical devices, face sophisticated adversarial threats that go beyond sensor spoofing. Pre-2026 security research has focused heavily on false data injection attacks on sensors, but attacks on actuators or the physical system dynamics (e.g., via component tampering or parameter corruption) are less studied and can be more devastating. This gap is critical for safety-critical systems where control performance and state estimation must be robust to such direct manipulations of the control loop.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified control-oriented identification and stochastic optimization framework can be designed to simultaneously detect, isolate, and compensate for adversarial corruption of both control inputs and time-varying system parameters, maintaining robust performance guarantees.
proposals.method: We will extend the Control-Oriented Identification via Stochastic Optimization framework to a two-stage process. First, we will develop a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) architecture, inspired by Hespanha's prior work on LuGre identification, to learn a nominal, high-fidelity model of the system dynamics and friction. Second, we will formulate a stochastic optimal control problem where adversarial perturbations to actuator commands and key physical parameters (e.g., mass, stiffness) are modeled as bounded, worst-case disturbances. A moving-horizon estimator, using the PINN as a prior, will perform joint state and attack vector estimation, informing a receding-horizon robust controller.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrated methodology for secure control that addresses a broader class of adversarial models. It will yield specific algorithms for attack-resilient estimation and control, with formal performance bounds. The results will be validated in simulation on high-precision mechatronic systems and a pharmacokinetic model of drug transport, demonstrating applicability to both mechanical and biomedical domains.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty lies in the combined focus on actuator and parameter corruption within a control-oriented identification framework, moving beyond sensor attacks. Integrating PINN-based high-fidelity modeling with stochastic optimization for real-time attack mitigation in this context is unexplored.

proposals.title: Robust Control-Oriented Identification Under Adversarial Perturbations and Stochastic Noise
proposals.motivation: Modern control systems, from autonomous vehicles to medical devices, must be designed using models identified from data that is often corrupted by both stochastic noise (e.g., sensor inaccuracy, process disturbances) and strategic adversarial perturbations (e.g., data poisoning, sensor spoofing). Pre-2026 research on adversarial robustness in machine learning often ignores the physical, stochastic dynamics central to control, while traditional stochastic system identification assumes benign noise, leaving systems vulnerable.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified identification framework that explicitly models both stochastic process/sensor noise and bounded adversarial perturbations will yield models with superior robustness and control performance guarantees compared to methods addressing only one disturbance type.
proposals.method: We will formulate a min-max stochastic optimization problem where the inner maximization finds worst-case adversarial perturbations within an L-infinity ball, and the outer minimization identifies system parameters (e.g., LuGre friction) to minimize a loss function under both this adversarial data and stochastic noise. The method builds on Hespanha's work on Control-Oriented Identification via Stochastic Optimization and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), using stochastic gradient descent with simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation (SPSA) to handle the non-convex, noisy objective. The adversarial perturbations will be constrained to be physically plausible, informed by the PINN's embedded physics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, practical algorithm for robust, control-oriented system identification that guarantees performance in mixed stochastic-adversarial environments. It will deliver explicit trade-off curves between nominal performance, stochastic noise rejection, and adversarial robustness, directly informing safer controller design.
proposals.novelty: The integration of adversarial attack models within a stochastic, physics-informed system identification framework is novel, moving beyond the typical separation between robust control (stochastic) and adversarial ML (deterministic) communities.

proposals.title: A Stochastic Optimization Framework for Attack-Aware Secure Control with Simultaneous Detection and Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Cyber-physical systems are increasingly vulnerable to sensor and actuator attacks that can bypass traditional fault detection and destabilize control loops. Pre-2026 secure control research often treats attack detection and resilient controller design as sequential or separate problems, leading to delayed response and suboptimal performance during stealthy or dynamic adversarial actions. This gap is critical in safety-sensitive domains like autonomous vehicles and medical devices, where timely mitigation is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating a unified, control-oriented identification problem that jointly optimizes a detection statistic and a control policy within a single stochastic optimization framework will yield superior closed-loop performance under attack compared to decoupled designs. This integrated approach will enable faster, more accurate mitigation.
proposals.method: We will develop a method combining a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) for learning attack dynamics and generating residual signals with a stochastic optimization core for joint estimation and control. The framework will treat the attack signal as an unknown disturbance to be estimated via a moving-horizon, sampling-based observer, inspired by Hespanha's prior work on control-oriented identification. This estimate will feed directly into a receding-horizon optimal controller that actively compensates for the inferred attack, using the stochastic optimization to balance performance loss and detection confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified architecture for secure control that closes the loop between detection and action. It will provide a rigorous, optimization-based methodology for trading off nominal performance and security, validated through high-fidelity simulations of networked control systems. The resulting framework will advance the design of autonomous systems capable of robust operation in adversarial environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, online integration of attack identification and mitigation via a single stochastic optimization problem, moving beyond the standard paradigm of a detector triggering a separate controller reconfiguration. This leverages control-oriented identification principles for security, a non-obvious synthesis of the researcher's themes.

proposals.title: Robust Vision-Based Control via Adaptive, Reliability-Weighted Sensor Fusion Inspired by Neuromotor State Estimation
proposals.motivation: Advanced robotic systems rely heavily on vision for feedback, but real-world visual data is often corrupted by noise, latency, and packet loss, degrading control performance. Classical control designs typically assume continuous, high-fidelity sensing, making them brittle in dynamic, uncertain environments. This problem mirrors the sensorimotor control challenge the brain solves during skilled movement, providing a rich biological analogy for robust engineering solutions.
proposals.hypothesis: A control architecture that explicitly models visual feedback as an intermittent, noisy measurement channel and dynamically adjusts state estimation weights based on online quality metrics will achieve superior robustness and performance compared to standard approaches that assume perfect sensing.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-oriented identification framework using stochastic optimization to learn a forward dynamics model and an observation model for the vision system. An adaptive Kalman filter variant (e.g., with innovation-based covariance matching) will serve as the state estimator, fusing predictions from the forward model with incoming visual measurements. The fusion weights (Kalman gains) will be dynamically tuned by an online sampling-based algorithm that estimates the instantaneous reliability of the visual feedback, quantified by its noise covariance and effective latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a principled, bio-inspired control design methodology for systems with highly uncertain sensing. It will provide formal stability and performance guarantees under intermittent observation, extending optimal control theory to more realistic sensing models. The resulting algorithms will be validated on a vision-based robotic manipulation task with induced sensor degradation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit, online coupling of sensory reliability estimation—inspired by neuromotor 'cue integration'—to the gain scheduling of a model-based controller, moving beyond treating state estimation and control as separate, static design problems in the face of variable sensor quality.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Policy Search for Adaptive Control: A Model-Free Approach Inspired by Virtual Drug Screening
proposals.motivation: Precise computed-torque control relies on accurate analytical models, which are difficult to obtain for robots with complex friction, flexibilities, or uncertain payloads. This model-dependence limits robust performance in real-world applications. Meanwhile, computational drug discovery successfully uses high-throughput virtual screening to find effective ligands without a full biochemical model, suggesting a powerful paradigm for bypassing complex system identification.
proposals.hypothesis: A model-free, high-throughput policy search algorithm can directly synthesize adaptive control policies that achieve robust performance without requiring an explicit, invertible analytical dynamics model, thereby outperforming model-based adaptive controllers in the presence of significant unmodeled dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a controller that treats the robot as a black-box function mapping policy parameters to a scalar performance metric (e.g., integrated squared tracking error). The core algorithm will be a Bayesian Optimization (BO) routine, chosen for its sample efficiency in optimizing expensive-to-evaluate functions. This BO loop will run in real-time or in high-fidelity simulation (e.g., MuJoCo), iteratively proposing and testing batches of policy parameter candidates (e.g., PID gains or simple neural network weights). The policy structure will be control-oriented but model-agnostic, focusing on task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for model-free adaptive control via direct policy search, reducing dependency on precise system identification. It will demonstrate, both in simulation and on a physical robotic testbed with significant friction and flexibility, that task performance can be achieved and adapted without an explicit dynamics model, offering a new tool for controlling poorly modeled systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual transfer of the high-throughput screening paradigm from drug discovery to adaptive control, explicitly rejecting the need for model inversion. While policy search exists in reinforcement learning, framing it as a sample-efficient, simulation-in-the-loop BO problem for direct adaptive control is a distinct, control-theoretic contribution.

proposals.title: Data-Driven Cooperative Control Under Joint Uncertainty in Agent Dynamics and Heterogeneous Communication Links
proposals.motivation: Multi-robot systems for tasks like search-and-rescue or environmental monitoring must operate reliably in unstructured environments. Pre-2026, cooperative control often assumes perfect knowledge of robot dynamics and communication networks, but real-world systems face significant uncertainties in both. Imperfect actuator models and heterogeneous, unknown wireless transmission powers (e.g., due to battery drain or hardware variance) can severely degrade formation stability and consensus performance, creating a critical gap between theory and deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, model-free learning framework that jointly adapts to uncertain local dynamics and infers the emergent, asymmetric communication graph from real-time signal measurements can guarantee robust performance in cooperative tasks where standard model-based controllers fail.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier stochastic optimization architecture. First, each agent employs a model-free reinforcement learning (RL) policy, such as a deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) method, to learn its local control law for the cooperative objective without a dynamics model. Concurrently, a recursive Bayesian estimation module will process received signal strength (RSSI) sequences to infer the relative transmission power—and thus the effective directed link gain—of each neighbor. This estimated communication adjacency matrix will be fed as a time-varying parameter into the RL critic's reward function, enabling the control policy to adapt to the learned network topology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, implementable framework for cooperative control that is robust to dual uncertainties, advancing the field of networked control systems. It will deliver new stability analysis tools for such coupled learning-estimation loops and experimentally validate the approach on a team of mobile robots with heterogeneous communication hardware.
proposals.novelty: While prior work treats communication as either perfect or a known stochastic process, and dynamics learning as separate, this proposal fuses the real-time, physics-informed identification of asymmetric communication power with model-free RL control into a single adaptive policy, a synthesis not present in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Control-Oriented, Sample-Efficient Adaptive Control via Physics-Informed Stochastic Optimization
proposals.motivation: High-performance computed-torque control for mobile robots relies on precise, often unattainable, dynamic models, limiting robustness in uncertain environments. Recent advances in Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for friction identification and stochastic optimization for control-oriented system identification offer a promising path to relax this dependency, but a unified framework for real-time, model-light adaptive control remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A control architecture combining a nominal model with a small, adaptive PINN-based disturbance estimator, trained online via a stochastic optimization loop, can achieve performance comparable to model-based computed-torque without requiring a full, high-fidelity dynamics model.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tiered controller: an outer-loop computed-torque law using a coarse nominal model, and an inner-loop adaptive compensator. The compensator will be a compact PINN, encoding prior physical structure (e.g., LuGre-like friction dynamics), that estimates the unmodeled dynamics. Its parameters will be updated online via a stochastic gradient descent algorithm, similar to Hespanha's control-oriented identification work, using real-time state and torque data. This ensures the learning is explicitly directed by a control performance metric, not just model fitting.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel adaptive control framework that is sample-efficient and retains interpretability through physics-informed learning. It will provide theoretical analysis on the stability and convergence of the coupled identification-control loop, and demonstrate superior tracking performance versus standard adaptive methods on a mobile robot platform with significant unmodeled dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of a physics-informed, parsimonious neural network as an adaptive element within a stochastic optimization loop explicitly designed for control performance, moving beyond pure model identification to direct performance-oriented learning.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control via Model-Free Policy Search, Inspired by Drug Discovery
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a precise computed-torque model is necessary for adaptive control by drawing an analogy from computational drug discovery. In that field, high-throughput virtual screening finds effective drug candidates by directly simulating molecular docking interactions, bypassing the need for an explicit analytical model of the complex biochemistry. Adapted to robotics, this suggests developing a controller that treats the robot's dynamics as a black box. The method would employ a model-free, high-throughput policy search algorithm (such as Bayesian Optimization) running in a loop with a dynamics simulator or the physical robot itself. The algorithm would directly evaluate candidate control policies based on a task performance metric (e.g., tracking error, energy use), iteratively adapting the policy parameters without ever constructing or inverting an analytical dynamics model, thereby creating robustness to model inaccuracies and uncertainties.

ideas.title: Stochastic Approximation with Sliding Mode for Robust Distributed Learning
ideas.description: This research synthesizes stochastic approximation theory with sliding mode control to develop a novel class of robust distributed learning algorithms. The core idea is to design the sliding surface dynamics as a stochastic approximation process, enabling formal analysis of convergence and stability in distributed settings with noisy gradients and communication delays. This approach provides theoretical guarantees for finite-time convergence to a bounded region despite persistent disturbances, which neither method achieves alone in distributed environments. The resulting framework bridges the gap between control-theoretic robustness and statistical learning theory for cooperative multi-agent systems.

ideas.title: Adapt Statistical Physics Mean-Field Methods for Distributed Stochastic Approximation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using analogical reasoning from statistical physics. The core challenge of formally analyzing a distributed stochastic approximation process—with many interacting agents causing complex, coupled stochastic dynamics—parallels the intractability of directly analyzing large systems of interacting particles. The analogous solution is mean-field theory, which simplifies the problem by approximating the aggregate interaction as an average external field, leading to a tractable self-consistent equation. Adapted to this research context, we propose modeling the distributed algorithm's update rule by separating it into a local stochastic approximation term and a deterministic mean-field term representing the network's average influence. This allows the algorithm's trajectory to be associated with a limiting mean-field ODE. The formal analysis then proceeds by proving the stability of this ODE and demonstrating that the stochastic process tracks its solution, thereby establishing convergence guarantees for the original distributed algorithm under specific network and data conditions.

ideas.title: Analytically-Guided Bayesian Bandits for Adaptive Distributed Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction integrates multi-armed bandit algorithms with analytical optimization models to create a hybrid, adaptive control system for distributed parameter selection. The analytical model is used to derive optimal prior distributions for bandit arm parameters (like step-sizes) and to shape the structure of the reward function and action space, injecting domain knowledge into the bandit's exploration. The bandit algorithm then operates on this informed, constrained space, dynamically learning and exploiting the best parameter choices in real-time based on a common reward signal, while the analytical model is continuously updated with bandit-derived data. This closed-loop synergy enables the system to provably adapt to changing network conditions or objective landscapes, balancing theoretical optimality with robust empirical performance.

ideas.title: Adaptive Control via Neuromotor-Inspired State Estimation
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from computational neuroscience on how the brain performs skilled movement despite imperfect sensory feedback. The analogous problem of sensorimotor control with noisy, delayed, and intermittent signals is solved biologically via internal models for prediction and reliability-weighted state estimation. Adapted to robotics, the idea is to design control laws that do not assume perfect, continuous target detection. Instead, they employ an internal forward model to generate state predictions, which are then fused with actual visual measurements using an adaptive filter (e.g., a Kalman filter variant). The fusion weights are dynamically adjusted based on online estimates of visual feedback quality (e.g., noise covariance, latency, or packet loss rate), making the control robust to the intermittent, noisy, or delayed outputs of a real-world vision system.

ideas.title: A Non-Asymptotic Heterogeneous-Agent SA Framework for Practical Distributed Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a new stochastic approximation (SA) framework tailored for the practical non-idealities of distributed learning algorithms. It moves beyond classical homogeneous assumptions to provide non-asymptotic convergence rates for systems with agent-specific data distributions, adaptive local stepsizes, and intermittent, delayed communications. The analysis yields explicit error decompositions separating optimization error from the costs of heterogeneity and asynchrony, enabling the design of adaptive tuning rules. This creates a robust theoretical foundation that both explains observed practical behaviors and provides actionable guidelines for algorithm configuration in real-world federated and decentralized settings.

ideas.title: Surrogate Model-Guided Intelligent Phase Perturbation Optimization
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the inefficiency of blind evolutionary search by implementing a model-based optimization loop for phase control. A surrogate model continuously learns the relationship between perturbation parameters and a target objective (e.g., yield, fidelity) from a sparse set of expensive experimental or simulated evaluations. An acquisition function, leveraging the model's predictions and uncertainty, then analytically selects the next perturbation to test, maximizing information gain per evaluation. This approach provides robust, sample-efficient convergence to optimal phase configurations by directly exploiting the smoothness and structure of the underlying physical landscape.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Learning with Strategically Corrupted One-Bit Feedback
ideas.description: This research develops online learning and bandit algorithms that are provably robust against an adaptive adversary who can strategically corrupt the one-bit feedback signal. The approach formulates a sequential game where the adversary, subject to a known corruption budget, can observe the algorithm's state and inject noise, delays, or losses to maximize regret. The core contribution is a novel algorithmic framework that achieves minimax optimal regret bounds by integrating robust statistics, careful bias-variance trade-offs in gradient estimates, and non-stochastic delay modeling. The resulting systems are applicable to security-critical and competitive environments where feedback cannot be assumed to be merely imperfect but may be maliciously manipulated.

ideas.title: Model-Free Cooperative Control for Mobile Robots with Heterogeneous and Unknown Communication Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a unified learning-based control framework for multi-robot systems that simultaneously overcomes two critical model uncertainties: imperfect robot dynamics and unknown, heterogeneous wireless transmission powers. The core idea is to design adaptive or reinforcement learning controllers that do not rely on a pre-computed dynamics model, while also integrating a real-time estimation process that infers the relative transmission powers of neighboring robots from signal measurements (e.g., RSSI). The learned communication model directly informs the cooperative control policy, allowing the robot team to achieve tasks like consensus or formation control by adapting to both physical interaction forces and the emergent, power-asymmetric communication network, all without explicit prior models for either.

ideas.title: Apply Sliding Mode Control with Immune System-Inspired Adaptive Switching
ideas.description: This idea adapts the robustness strategy of biological immune systems to sliding mode control (SMC) design for tracking under uncertainty. In immunology, robustness is achieved through a hierarchical response: a fast, general innate reaction to any 'danger signal,' followed by a slower, specific adaptive response. The analogous solution is translated into a dual-mode SMC architecture. A primary sliding surface with a high-gain, discontinuous control term acts as the 'innate' response, ensuring immediate robustness when tracking error (the danger signal) is detected. Concurrently, an online disturbance observer or parameter estimator functions as the 'adaptive' layer, learning the specific characteristics of the model uncertainties and disturbances. The control signal is then synthesized from both layers, potentially using a blending function that reduces reliance on the chattering-prone innate term as the adaptive estimate improves. This biologically-inspired adaptation aims to preserve the classic robustness of SMC while mitigating chattering and improving performance against persistent, structured uncertainties.

ideas.title: Validate control laws using a hierarchical, simulation-to-reality framework inspired by drug discovery
ideas.description: This expanded idea proposes to validate the proposed image-based control laws using a hierarchical, simulation-to-reality framework inspired by phased clinical trials in drug discovery. The analogous solution involves a staged pipeline from in silico simulation to in vivo testing. Adapted to robotics, the research would involve creating a multi-tiered validation protocol: first, exhaustive testing in high-fidelity, randomized simulations modeling dynamic, cluttered, and outdoor conditions; second, physical testing in a lab with introduced variability and perturbations; and finally, controlled field tests. This approach systematically builds confidence in the control laws' robustness while managing the risks and costs associated with direct testing in complex, unpredictable environments.

ideas.title: Meta-Learned Adaptive Correlation for Perturbation-Based Optimization
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond assuming simple correlation to actively learning the optimal structure for coordinated phase perturbations. A meta-learner, trained on a suite of representative optimization problems, discovers a policy that maps current state (e.g., gradient history, estimated Hessian information) to a covariance matrix for perturbations. This adaptive correlation mechanism ensures perturbations are constructive, aligning with favorable directions in the loss landscape to escape saddles and navigate ravines. The result is a robust, generalizable acceleration strategy that provably reduces the number of iterations to convergence without the instability risks of ad-hoc coordination.

ideas.title: Stability-Guaranteed, Meta-Learning-Augmented Adaptive Control with Imprecise Model Priors
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid adaptive control framework that guarantees stability and safety while minimizing reliance on a precise robot dynamics model. It combines a nominal, simplified analytical model with a meta-learned adaptive component initialized from broad offline data, enabling rapid, data-efficient online adaptation to the specific robot and task. Crucially, the online learning is constrained by formal stability criteria, such as Lyapunov conditions or control barrier functions, ensuring robust performance even during the initial learning phase. The result is a practical, certifiably safe learning-based controller suitable for real-world mobile robots.

ideas.title: Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) Estimator for Agile Target Tracking
ideas.description: This system employs an Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) framework to robustly estimate the state of a target exhibiting complex, switching dynamics. It runs multiple Kalman filters concurrently, each based on a distinct motion model, and uses a Bayesian algorithm to compute the probability that each model is currently correct. The final state estimate and covariance are a probabilistically weighted combination of the outputs from all filters, enabling seamless adaptation to maneuvers. This design maintains smoothing benefits while drastically reducing lag and error during unpredictable target motion, providing a more reliable input for the downstream controller.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Convergence Analysis for Scalable Multi-Transmitter Systems
ideas.description: This research fuses convergence analysis with event-triggered control to develop a novel analytical framework for distributed multi-transmitter systems. The core objective is to characterize how the event-triggering threshold and the frequency of control updates directly impact the algorithm's convergence rate as the number of transmitters scales. This allows for the joint optimization of the triggering mechanism and control law to guarantee a desired convergence speed while minimizing computational load. The outcome is a set of co-design principles and scaling laws that specify how to adjust event thresholds with system size to maintain performance, a capability neither pure analysis nor pure implementation provides alone.

ideas.title: Analyze convergence scaling via statistical physics models of interacting particle systems
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solution methods from statistical physics to analyze the convergence speed of a multi-transmitter algorithm. The analogous problem is the convergence to equilibrium in large systems of interacting particles, such as spin models or flocking agents, where scaling laws are derived using mean-field theory and finite-size analysis. By modeling transmitters as interacting agents within an interference graph, we will apply these techniques to characterize the algorithm's convergence rate as a function of the number of transmitters N. The adapted approach will derive theoretical scaling laws (e.g., convergence time ~ N^α) and identify critical points (e.g., in transmit power or node density) where convergence behavior undergoes a phase transition, providing fundamental limits and design guidelines for large-scale networks.

ideas.title: Adapting Synthetic Biology's Distributed Sensing for Nano-Robot Vision
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from synthetic biology, where engineered cells perform complex sensing with minimal, distributed molecular components, to the challenge of vision-based control on nano-robots. The analogous solution uses stochastic, event-triggered molecular logic rather than centralized, high-fidelity processing. The adapted idea is to implement a vision-based tracking control architecture on a resource-constrained nano-robot platform by replacing traditional computer vision with a distributed, event-driven optical sensing system. This system would use a sparse sensor array to detect simple, coded light patterns from the environment or a beacon, reducing on-board computation to minimal pattern matching and triggering efficient, pre-defined motion responses, thereby enabling autonomous tracking within severe size, power, and computational constraints.

ideas.title: Robust Vision-Based Navigation in Unstructured, Dynamic Environments with Imperfect Perception
ideas.description: This research investigates the co-design of perception-aware control laws and state estimation for robotic systems operating in complex, dynamic, and cluttered outdoor environments, where visual feedback is inherently noisy, intermittent, and delayed. The core challenge is to develop controllers that do not merely tolerate imperfect perception but dynamically adapt their guidance and exploration strategies based on a real-time confidence model of the vision system's output. This involves leveraging environmental structure and predictable dynamics (e.g., target motion models, scene semantics) to inform robust prediction and recovery during perceptual dropouts or corruption. The outcome will be a unified framework that achieves reliable navigation and task completion in settings where neither a pristine lab environment nor perfect, continuous vision can be assumed.

ideas.title: Adaptive Portfolio Selection via Cooperative Multi-Armed Bandits
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cooperative multi-armed bandit solution from computational finance's portfolio optimization problem to the context of distributed phase selection. In the analogous field, multiple agents explore financial assets with uncertain returns, sharing reward information to optimize a collective portfolio. The adapted research idea frames each node in a distributed computing system as an agent in a cooperative bandit problem. The 'arms' are the available computational phases or tasks, and the common reward signal is a shared performance metric (e.g., throughput, accuracy). By implementing bandit algorithms that facilitate information sharing (like gossip-based UCB or Thompson sampling), the distributed system can dynamically and efficiently allocate resources to the most promising phases, minimizing total completion time or maximizing overall output quality in the face of uncertainty.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Robust Optimization under Imperfect One-Bit Feedback
ideas.description: This research investigates the co-evolution of optimization algorithms and their feedback interpretation mechanisms under realistic, imperfect one-bit feedback conditions. An evolutionary algorithm framework is used not just to guide phase perturbations, but to evolve the entire decision policy, including how to interpret noisy, delayed, or lost binary signals. This allows the system to discover robust strategies that implicitly model and compensate for the feedback channel's imperfections. The core novelty is the closed-loop adaptation where the evolutionary process learns to extract reliable gradient or ranking information from corrupted feedback, leading to algorithms that are both high-performing and inherently fault-tolerant.

ideas.title: Correlated Phase Perturbations via Flocking Synchronization Models
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption of independent phase adjustments by investigating correlated or coordinated phase perturbations. The analogy is drawn from collective animal behavior, specifically flocking synchronization models, where individuals achieve rapid global coordination through simple local interaction rules rather than independent action. The analogous solution involves applying principles like those in the Vicsek model, where an agent's heading is adjusted based on the average heading of its neighbors. Adapted to the research context, this means designing phase perturbation mechanisms where the adjustment for each element is a function of the phases and gradient information of a dynamically defined subset of other elements. This creates intentional, problem-structure-informed correlations in the perturbations, which could allow the optimization process to escape local minima more effectively and converge faster than with independent, random phase adjustments.

ideas.description: This research direction focuses on building a physical robotic platform where a perception-agnostic controller is subjected to real, co-occurring impairments. The hardware testbed will deliberately introduce controlled, realistic RF impairments (like latency and packet loss) to the communication channel carrying visual feedback, which is itself noisy and intermittent. This allows for the experimental validation of control laws that do not assume perfect, timely detection, but instead must maintain stability and performance using fused, stale, or missing state estimates. The core novelty is the holistic, hardware-based investigation of robustness at the intersection of unreliable sensing and unreliable data links, moving beyond simulated or isolated impairments.

ideas.title: Analytically Optimized Distributed Phase Locking via Adaptive Consensus
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes analytical optimization of algorithmic parameters with distributed consensus for phase synchronization. It develops a closed-loop system where an analytical model of the consensus dynamics informs the real-time, optimal adjustment of step-sizes (e.g., coupling gains) and feedback strategies across the network. This allows the system to analytically derive and enforce the parameter distributions that minimize convergence time or energy use for phase locking, rather than relying on static or heuristic choices. Consequently, the network achieves provably optimal synchronization that is robust to local disturbances and changing network topologies, a capability beyond what either analytical optimization in isolation or standard consensus algorithms can provide.

ideas.title: Multi-Armed Bandit Visual Servoing for Adaptive Multi-Camera Control
ideas.description: This research fuses visual servoing with multi-armed bandits to develop an adaptive multi-camera tracking controller. The system frames each available camera (or feature set from a camera) as an 'arm' in a contextual bandit problem, where the context is the current state of the target and the environment. The common reward signal is derived from the visual servoing error or task completion efficiency. This allows the controller to learn online which camera feed provides the most reliable information for the servoing task, dynamically switching or fusing inputs to maintain robust performance despite occlusions, lighting changes, or target maneuvers.

ideas.title: Adaptive Predictive Beamforming with Dual-Rate Channel Dynamics Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a distributed beamforming system that actively learns and predicts time-varying channel dynamics to maintain coherence. It employs an adaptive dual-rate estimation framework, where a fast Kalman filter tracks rapid phase fluctuations and a slower, robust filter estimates gain variations and channel model parameters. The system autonomously adjusts its prediction horizon and update rate based on real-time estimates of the channel's Doppler spectrum and observed prediction errors, ensuring stability. This closed-loop, model-aware approach provides robust performance across a wide range of mobility scenarios and channel coherence times.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Control for Navigation via Immune System-Inspired Affinity Modulation
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from computational immunology. The immune system elegantly solves the analogous problem of tracking specific pathogens while avoiding damage to healthy self-tissue through a hierarchical, affinity-based control system. We adapt this solution to robotic navigation by proposing a dual-layer controller architecture. A high-level 'adaptive tracking' module, akin to the adaptive immune response, is dedicated to high-precision, model-based tracking of a dynamic target object. Concurrently, a low-level 'regulatory avoidance' module, inspired by immune tolerance mechanisms, acts as a reactive system that generates repulsive or inhibitory signals for all perceived obstacles. The core innovation is an affinity-modulation mechanism that dynamically weights and combines these two command streams. The tracking command's gain or 'affinity' is continuously attenuated based on the strength and immediacy of the avoidance signal, allowing for smooth, prioritized transitions between tracking and obstacle avoidance behaviors, thereby enabling safe and robust navigation in cluttered, dynamic environments.

ideas.title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Phase Alignment with Shared Priors
ideas.description: A hybrid architecture is proposed where a central meta-controller learns a robust initialization for phase adjustment policies from aggregated, anonymized experience across all transmitters. This shared prior, which captures generalizable strategies for interference management, is periodically distributed to the network nodes. Each transmitter then uses a local, lightweight reinforcement learning algorithm to rapidly adapt this prior policy to its specific spatial location and unique feedback history. This approach drastically reduces convergence time, ensures stable learning by providing a strong baseline, and maintains scalability as execution remains fully decentralized.

ideas.title: Neuromorphic Event-Triggered Control for Visual Processing
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience and neuromorphic engineering. The analogous solution is the operation of event-based vision sensors, where pixels asynchronously transmit data only upon detecting a significant change in luminance, minimizing data flow and power use. This principle is adapted to create a neuromorphic-inspired event-triggered control system for visual processing. A 'significance function'—measuring visual change through metrics like optical flow or feature displacement—is continuously monitored at a low cost. Full, computationally expensive control updates (e.g., model predictive control, high-dimensional state estimation) are triggered only when this function exceeds a threshold, thereby reducing the average computational load on the PC by avoiding redundant processing during periods of visual stability.

ideas.title: Prove Phase Distribution via Central Limit Theorem and Large Deviation Theory
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the rigorous probabilistic framework used in statistical physics to validate the conjectured distribution of received phases in a large-N transmitter system. The analogous problem—deriving macroscopic thermodynamic properties from microscopic random interactions—is solved by combining the Central Limit Theorem for typical fluctuations with Large Deviation Theory for rare events. The adapted solution involves two key steps: 1) Prove that the normalized sum of many independent (or weakly correlated) complex phasors converges in distribution to a complex Gaussian random variable, validating the core of the analytical model for typical phase values. 2) Rigorously establish a Large Deviation Principle for the received phase, deriving the precise rate function that governs the exponentially small probability of large deviations from the mean, thus proving the conjecture for the entire distribution, including tail behavior. This approach provides a complete mathematical foundation for the statistical mechanics analogy used in the original conjecture.

ideas.title: Robust Analytical-Optimal Hybrids via Stress-Testing and Adaptation
ideas.description: This approach first uses the analytical model to generate candidate optimal parameter strategies. These candidates are then subjected to an adversarial stress-testing phase against a curated zoo of non-ideal problem characteristics, such as sharp minima, saddle points, and non-stationary noise. A lightweight online meta-learning layer monitors application performance, detecting signs of assumption violation (e.g., exploding gradients, stagnation). Finally, the system dynamically interpolates between the pure analytical policy and fallback robust strategies, ensuring stable performance even when theoretical guarantees break down.

ideas.title: Robust Probabilistic Vision for Occluded Target Tracking via Adaptive Kalman Filtering
ideas.description: This research develops a tightly integrated perception-estimation framework where a robust, adaptive vision processing module and a Kalman filter operate in a bidirectional feedback loop. The Kalman filter provides not only smoothed state estimates but also real-time predictive uncertainty metrics (covariance) that dynamically reconfigure the vision algorithm—adjusting regions of interest, feature matching tolerances, and detection confidence thresholds—especially during partial occlusion or lighting transitions. This allows the vision system to remain probabilistically 'locked on' to a target by focusing computational resources on the most likely target states, recovering measurements more reliably when the target re-emerges. The result is a significant improvement in tracking continuity and robustness for autonomous systems in dynamic, cluttered environments compared to using either robust vision or filtering alone.

ideas.title: Control under Perceptual Distrust: Resilient Laws for Contested or Deceptive Sensing
ideas.description: This research designs control laws that maintain stability and task progress not just under noisy or delayed vision, but when visual feedback is actively misleading or untrustworthy. The core innovation is a perception-aware control framework that quantifies the credibility of the visual state estimate in real-time by checking its consistency with other sensor streams and internal model predictions. The controller seamlessly interpolates between vision-centric operation and vision-agnostic fallback strategies based on this perceived trust, ensuring robustness to both benign sensor faults and adversarial perceptual attacks.

ideas.title: Multi-Fidelity Characterization of Conditional Convergence Scaling Under Heterogeneous Real-World Constraints
ideas.description: This research develops a comprehensive framework to characterize algorithm convergence not as a simple function of transmitter count, but as a conditional scaling law dependent on critical system parameters. It employs a tiered methodology: first establishing fundamental theoretical bounds, then performing controlled hardware-in-the-loop experiments to isolate specific impairment effects, and finally executing high-fidelity simulations across a broad synthetic parameter space capturing heterogeneous channel states, interference regimes, and hardware imperfections. The outcome is a predictive model that maps convergence behavior across this multi-dimensional space, providing robust, context-dependent scaling insights far more applicable to real-world deployment than a single idealized curve.

ideas.title: Consensus-Driven Correlated Perturbations for Superlinear Phase Synchronization
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes distributed consensus protocols for phase alignment with the deliberate design of correlated phase perturbations. Instead of treating perturbations as independent noise to be averaged out, the consensus logic is used to compute and coordinate specific, structured phase kicks across subsets of oscillators. These correlated adjustments are designed to exploit the network's topology and current state, pushing the system collectively over potential barriers or through low-dimensional manifolds towards consensus. The result is a closed-loop, algorithmic control strategy where the consensus process itself learns to generate accelerating 'shoves', achieving superlinear convergence rates impossible with either independent adjustments or standard consensus alone.

ideas.title: Adaptive Evasion via Immunological T Cell Strategies
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, specifically the evasion mechanisms of Cytotoxic T cells within immunosuppressive tumor environments, this research investigates control strategies for a target robot to evade a vision-based pursuer. The analogous solution involves T cells adaptively editing their receptor sensitivity to evade suppression by deceptive signals from cancer cells. Adapted to robotics, the idea is to develop a control system where the target robot employs an adaptive internal model to dynamically reinterpret the visual cues from the pursuer, effectively 'tuning' its threat perception to ignore predictable pursuit patterns and execute counter-intuitive evasion trajectories. This transforms the evasion problem from a direct reactive task into one of adaptive signal interpretation and strategic deception-breaking.

ideas.title: Adaptive Saccadic Control for Object Tracking with a Narrow-FOV Camera
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from the human visual system—which operates without omni-directional vision—to the problem of object tracking with a standard, limited field-of-view camera. The analogous biological solution uses predictive saccadic eye movements, planned using peripheral cues and internal motion models, to reposition the high-acuity fovea, followed by smooth pursuit. The adapted idea implements a dual-mode estimation and control strategy for a robotic camera: a 'saccadic' controller uses a learned motion model and processing of low-resolution peripheral image regions to trigger and execute rapid, open-loop camera re-pointing actions to re-acquire a target that is about to leave or has left the FOV. A 'pursuit' controller then engages for precise, feedback-driven tracking. The core challenge is developing the predictive model that minimizes the blind period during camera movement, effectively creating a virtual wide-area tracking system from a narrow sensor.

ideas.title: Cooperative Vision-Based Tracking with a Swarm of Resource-Constrained Micro/Nano Robots
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the design of a vision-based cooperative control architecture for a swarm of micro- or nano-scale mobile robots to track dynamic targets. The core challenge is to develop ultra-lightweight communication, distributed state estimation, and formation control algorithms that can run on the severe computational, memory, and power constraints of each tiny robot. The system would leverage the swarm's large numbers and redundancy to achieve robust, adaptive, and precise tracking in environments like fluidic channels, biological tissues, or intricate machinery, where single robots fail and larger robots cannot operate. Success would demonstrate a new paradigm for minimally invasive monitoring and intervention at the smallest scales.

ideas.title: Adaptive Phase Control via Multi-Agent Market Bidding Policies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from smart grid energy markets, where distributed energy resources use reinforcement learning to develop autonomous bidding policies based on market feedback. In the analogous communications problem, each transmitter is an agent that learns an individual policy. This policy maps the agent's history of local feedback (e.g., SINR, ACK/NACK) to precise phase and amplitude adjustments for its signal. The system creates a virtual 'performance market' where the common feedback signal (like the resulting combined signal quality) acts as a price, guiding decentralized agents. By selfishly optimizing their perceived utility from this feedback, their independent actions collectively converge to an optimal global state, such as coherent beamforming, achieving robust and adaptive distributed transmission without a central controller dictating phases.

ideas.title: Joint Calibration-Free Localization and Transmit Power Inference via Constrained Ratio Analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses the identifiability crisis of heterogeneous unknown powers by moving from direct ranging to a constrained likelihood model. It exploits the fact that the ratio of received signal strengths from two transmitters at a single receiver cancels out the receiver's unknown gain and much of the path loss geometry, creating a solvable constraint. The framework jointly estimates locations and transmit powers by combining these ratio constraints across the network with a pragmatic prior on transmit powers—modeling them as drawn from a finite set corresponding to common device types—or by using a minimal subset of anchors with calibrated power. This enables stable localization without prior power calibration, overcoming the fundamental degeneracy of the original formulation.

ideas.title: Optical Field Steering for Computation-Free Micro-Robot Control
ideas.description: This research designs a control architecture where micro or nano robots are guided using an externally generated, dynamic optical field, eliminating the need for on-board vision processing. The robots are equipped with basic photodiodes to sense the field, while their position is tracked by an overhead camera system. A central controller uses the tracking data to modulate the optical field in real-time, creating attractive or repulsive zones that directly steer the robots toward desired trajectories. This approach enables precise vision-based control of resource-constrained platforms by outsourcing all complex computation to the infrastructure.

ideas.title: Active Gaze Control with Probabilistic World Modeling for Robust Monocular Tracking
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fatal flaw of object loss in narrow-FOV tracking by combining estimation and active perception. A probabilistic filter maintains a multi-hypothesis belief state of the target's dynamics and possible occluders, creating a persistent world model. An actuator-controlled camera is dynamically redirected by a policy trained to optimize a dual objective: maintaining target observation and strategically exploring high-uncertainty regions when the target is lost. This creates a robust closed-loop perception-action cycle where the camera's limited view is actively managed as a resource, enabling recovery from occlusions and prolonged tracking in complex scenes.

ideas.title: Dual-Threshold Event-Triggered Control with Model-Based Stability Guarantees for Visual Systems
ideas.description: This system reduces computational load by executing full control updates only when necessary, but it rigorously avoids instability by using two triggering conditions. A fast visual saliency check identifies significant scene changes, while a parallel, lightweight model-based state observer runs at a minimal fixed rate to predict critical latent variables. An update is forced if the prediction error for these stability-critical states exceeds a theoretically derived bound, preventing performance degradation during visually subtle but dynamically important events. This ensures computational savings while formally maintaining system stability and tracking performance.
