ideas.title: Deep Visual Predictive Servoing with Occlusion-Robust Feature Learning
ideas.description: This system overcomes the fragility of classical visual servoing by merging model-based control with learned visual representations. A CNN is trained offline to extract persistent, high-level features from the target object that are resilient to partial occlusion and lighting variance. These features feed a visual predictive controller that uses a learned model of target and ego-motion dynamics to optimize control inputs over a receding horizon. This results in a tracking controller that is robust to real-world perceptual disturbances and can anticipate motion for smoother, more reliable pursuit.

ideas.title: Reinforcement Learning for Distributed Beamforming in a Camera-Guided Mobile Robot Network
ideas.description: This research synthesizes decentralized reinforcement learning for phase adjustment with a stationary camera-based sensing architecture. A network of fixed cameras tracks a mobile robot, providing its real-time position as a global performance metric to a set of distributed, single-antenna transmitters. Each transmitter runs a local RL agent that learns a policy to map its own history of received feedback (derived from the camera-measured signal strength at the robot) to precise phase adjustments, enabling the emergent, self-organized formation of a coherent beam that follows the robot. This creates a robust wireless power transfer or communication system that autonomously adapts to mobility and blockages by fusing high-level visual guidance with low-level distributed RF coordination.

ideas.title: Adaptive Kalman Filtering with Dynamic Process Noise from Geophysics
ideas.description: This idea expands the original Kalman filter application by incorporating an adaptive mechanism inspired by seismic signal processing. In geophysics, adaptive Kalman filters dynamically estimate process noise to handle non-stationary background seismic activity. Adapted to visual target state estimation, this involves implementing an online algorithm (e.g., using innovation-based covariance matching) to continuously estimate and update the process noise covariance matrix (Q) of the Kalman filter. This allows the estimator to automatically increase prediction uncertainty during aggressive target maneuvers and tighten it during steady motion, leading to smoother and more accurate tracking without manual parameter tuning for different motion regimes.

ideas.title: Event-Triggered Control with a Predictive Kalman Filter for Efficient Visual Servoing
ideas.description: This research direction combines event-triggered control with a Kalman filter to create a predictive, resource-efficient visual servoing system. The Kalman filter continuously runs a low-cost prediction step to estimate the target's state and, crucially, to forecast the future innovation (measurement residual). A control update is triggered only when this predicted innovation exceeds a threshold, indicating a significant future deviation, not just a past one. This allows the system to proactively schedule computations, maintain smooth control via the filter's state estimate during non-triggered periods, and drastically reduce processing load compared to traditional frame-by-frame visual servoing.

ideas.title: Analytically Optimize Step-Size and Feedback via Optical Coating Design Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from thin-film optics. In optical coating design, analytical models (transfer-matrix method) are used to derive optimal layer thicknesses and sequences to achieve a desired light interference pattern, avoiding brute-force search. We adapt this solution to the context of optimizing parameters for an iterative algorithm. The algorithm's analytical model is used to frame the progression of error as a wave-like interference process. By treating each iteration as a layer with specific parameters (step-size, feedback gain), we can set up and solve an analytical optimization condition—akin to designing for constructive/destructive interference—to derive closed-form or semi-analytical formulas for the optimal step-size distribution and feedback strategy sequence. This provides a principled, model-based alternative to heuristic tuning or exhaustive numerical hyperparameter optimization.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Search for Optimal Collective Phase Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the investigation of correlated phase perturbations with evolutionary algorithm strategies. It treats entire patterns of coordinated phase adjustments across a system as 'genomes' to be evolved. A genetic algorithm uses selection pressure based on convergence acceleration and mutation/crossover operators to explore the space of possible correlation structures between phase variables. This allows the system to autonomously discover complex, emergent coordination schemes that would be difficult to derive analytically, potentially uncovering novel phase dynamics that fundamentally reshape the optimization landscape.

ideas.title: Adaptive Visual Tracking via Predator-Prey Pursuit Dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from the field of Behavioral Ecology, specifically predator-prey pursuit dynamics, to scale up the problem of ground vehicle tracking using onboard cameras. The analogous solution involves predators using predictive models of prey behavior for interception, not just reactive chasing. The adapted idea is to develop an autonomous tracking system where the ego-vehicle acts as a 'predator,' fusing real-time visual data from cameras to construct and update a dynamic model of the 'prey' target vehicle's behavior. This model would anticipate maneuvers based on visual cues like vehicle orientation, relative velocity, and road geometry, enabling the tracker to compute and execute a predictive, rather than purely reactive, pursuit trajectory for more robust and efficient tracking in complex urban or highway environments.

ideas.title: Dual+: Fault-Tolerant Hybrid Localization with Stationary Cameras and Onboard Fallback
ideas.description: This system employs a fixed camera network for primary localization but is designed to handle inevitable coverage gaps. It uses a probabilistic fusion framework to combine high-accuracy, intermittent global poses from the cameras with a continuously running, drift-prone onboard state estimate. When external vision is available, it corrects the onboard estimate; when it is not, the robot operates autonomously using its onboard sensors, with the system quantifying the resulting localization uncertainty. This creates a robust guidance system that maintains functionality across both covered and uncovered environments, inverting the sensing architecture without creating a single point of failure.

ideas.title: Unified Predictive Control for Occlusion-Aware Tracking and Navigation
ideas.description: This system employs a model predictive controller with a multi-objective cost function that jointly optimizes for target tracking, obstacle avoidance, and the maintenance of target visibility. It predicts future states of both the target and dynamic obstacles to plan trajectories that avoid collisions while strategically positioning the agent to prevent the target from becoming occluded. The framework formally handles the trade-off between close tracking and safe navigation, enabling behaviors like temporary lag to skirt an obstacle before reacquiring an optimal tracking position. This results in robust, goal-oriented navigation in complex, cluttered, and dynamic environments.

ideas.title: Robust Online Learning via Error-Correcting Feedback Codes
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of perfect single-bit feedback in online learning or bandit algorithms. Drawing an analogy from digital communications, where error-correcting codes protect bits over noisy channels, we propose to encode the one-bit feedback signal. The solution is to design a protocol where the feedback for each algorithmic round is not a single bit but a carefully constructed, redundant codeword. The algorithm must then incorporate a decoding step, using techniques inspired by Hamming codes or low-density parity-check codes, to robustly reconstruct the intended feedback from a noisy, delayed, or partially observed codeword. This shifts the research focus to co-designing feedback encoding schemes and robust decoding algorithms to maintain performance under imperfect feedback conditions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Distributed Beamforming using Predictive Control and Feedback, inspired by Robotic Swarm Formation
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending distributed beamforming algorithms to time-varying channel models by drawing an analogy from swarm robotics, where maintaining dynamic formations is a solved problem. The analogous solution uses predictive models and continuous local feedback for each agent. Adapting this, the research idea is to develop a beamforming algorithm where each transmitting node employs a predictive model for local channel evolution and combines it with periodic, low-rate feedback. This creates a dual proactive-reactive control loop, allowing the distributed array to maintain coherent beamforming despite time-varying gains and phase offsets, significantly improving performance in mobile or dynamic scattering environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive Higher-Order Sliding Mode Control with Disturbance Observer for Chattering-Free Robust Tracking
ideas.description: This research designs a robust tracking controller that overcomes the chattering problem of conventional sliding mode control. The core employs a second-order super-twisting sliding mode algorithm to provide continuous control effort while maintaining robustness to matched uncertainties. An adaptive law dynamically adjusts the control gains based on a real-time estimate of the uncertainty bounds, preventing excessive gain conservatism. Finally, a nonlinear disturbance observer is integrated to estimate and compensate for disturbances feedforwardly, further reducing the magnitude of the discontinuous correction needed and ensuring smooth, precise tracking performance.

ideas.title: Apply Ant Colony Optimization for Phase Perturbation Pathfinding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from swarm intelligence in operations research—specifically Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) for pathfinding—to the problem of guiding phase perturbations. The analogous ACO solution uses simulated ants that deposit pheromones on paths, with successful, shorter paths attracting more ants, enabling efficient convergence on optimal routes. Adapted to the research context, artificial agents propose incremental phase changes, and the quality of the resulting system state (e.g., energy, order parameter) acts as a reinforcing 'pheromone,' building a probabilistic map that guides subsequent perturbations. This swarm-based, stigmergic approach offers a decentralized, adaptive alternative to genetic algorithm operators for exploring and optimizing in complex phase spaces.

ideas.title: Apply Byzantine Fault-Tolerant Consensus for Robust Phase Locking in Adversarial Environments
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from distributed computing—specifically, Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus algorithms used in secure blockchain and distributed systems—to the problem of phase locking in oscillator networks. The analogous solution involves protocols that allow a distributed system to reach agreement despite malicious or arbitrarily faulty components. Adapted to this context, each oscillator (or phase estimator) would not only exchange phase information with its neighbors but also participate in a local consensus round, treating incoming phases as proposed values. By employing a BFT voting mechanism, the network can identify and isolate outliers, enabling it to converge to a consistent phase even when a bounded number of nodes are adversarial, highly noisy, or defective. This creates a method for robust synchronization resilient to faults and attacks, transferring the core principle of fault-tolerant agreement from computing to dynamical systems.

ideas.title: Distributed Multi-Target Beamforming with Limited Field-of-View Visual Tracking
ideas.description: This research fuses the challenge of tracking objects with a limited field-of-view camera with the problem of distributed beamforming to multiple receivers. It investigates how a team of mobile agents, each equipped with a standard narrow-view camera and a directional transmitter, can collaboratively illuminate a dynamic set of targets that are not all visible to any single agent. The core synergy is the development of joint estimation and control algorithms where partial, uncertain visual tracks from individual agents are fused in real-time to compute and adapt distributed beamforming weights, enabling effective multi-target radio or optical energy delivery despite the collective sensory constraint of incomplete omni-directional awareness.

ideas.title: Communication-Robust Decentralized Multi-Robot Target Tracking with Intermittent Interaction
ideas.description: This research develops a vision-based multi-robot tracking system designed to function reliably under real-world communication constraints. Instead of relying on a central coordinator, it employs a decentralized control architecture where each robot maintains a local belief state about target positions and teammate intentions, shared only intermittently via a bandwidth-efficient protocol. The core innovation is a distributed estimation and task allocation algorithm that guarantees collision avoidance and tracking coverage stability even during prolonged communication dropouts. The system is validated in complex, cluttered environments to demonstrate robustness against the dynamic network conditions that typically break naive scaling approaches.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Fault Injection and Attribution for Image-Based Control in Parameterized Adversarial Worlds
ideas.description: This research moves beyond passive testing by developing a closed-loop system that actively diagnoses the causes of control failure in complex visual environments. A simulation framework generates environments with systematically varied adversarial parameters (dynamic clutter, lighting gradients, motion blur, and camouflage) while instrumenting the control policy's perceptual and decision-making pipeline. Each failure is automatically attributed to specific perceptual confusions or control delays, guiding a targeted redesign of the control law's visual processing or temporal reasoning components. This results in control schemes that are not just tested in complexity, but are iteratively hardened against diagnosed failure modes.

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Modal Perception for All-Weather Vehicle-to-Vehicle Tracking
ideas.description: This research develops a resilient tracking system for autonomous vehicles that fuses camera, radar, and lidar data to maintain a reliable estimate of a target vehicle's state under diverse and challenging environmental conditions. A core innovation is a learned, context-aware fusion module that dynamically weights sensor inputs based on real-time estimates of their individual reliability (e.g., reducing camera weight in fog, increasing radar weight for direct range). The system is trained and evaluated on a comprehensive synthetic and real-world dataset encompassing edge-case scenarios like sensor dropout, partial occlusion, and extreme weather to ensure robustness where single-modality systems would fail.

ideas.title: Distributed Multi-Robot Tracking via Swarm Intelligence Algorithms
ideas.description: This research expands the vision-based control architecture for multi-robot cooperative tracking by drawing an analogy from computational biology and swarm intelligence. The analogous problem—how decentralized biological swarms collectively track or encircle targets—is solved using local interaction rules and stigmergic communication. The adapted idea proposes a decentralized control system where each mobile robot, equipped with vision sensors, follows a set of local behavioral rules inspired by swarm models (like separation, alignment, and cohesion relative to the target and teammates). Coordination emerges through local visual detection and communication via a shared environmental map, enabling scalable, fault-tolerant cooperative tracking of single or multiple dynamic targets without relying on a central controller.

ideas.title: Implement and test in real hardware using a phased validation approach from biomedical device development
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from biomedical device development, this expanded idea proposes adapting phased validation protocols used for medical devices to RF algorithm hardware testing. Medical device developers systematically progress from bench testing to animal models to limited human trials before full clinical deployment, identifying real-world complexities at each stage while managing risk. Adapted to this research context, the solution involves creating a structured hardware validation protocol: first testing the RF algorithm in a controlled lab with programmable impairment generators (bench equivalent), then in a semi-controlled testbed with real but calibrated hardware (animal model equivalent), and finally in an operational environment with full real-world impairments (clinical trial equivalent). This phased approach systematically identifies which RF impairments matter most at each fidelity level, allowing for targeted algorithm adjustments before committing to full prototype deployment, thereby reducing development risk and cost while ensuring the algorithm is robust against the specific impairments present in real hardware.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Optimization of Viewpoint Selection and Motion for Occlusion-Aware Object Tracking
ideas.description: This research proposes using an evolutionary algorithm to optimize not just the internal estimation parameters of a monocular object tracker, but also to generate and select high-level viewpoint motion strategies for the camera platform itself. The algorithm's population consists of candidate solutions encoding both filter parameters and suggested camera maneuvers (e.g., pan-left, move-right to clear occlusion). Fitness is evaluated based on tracking accuracy and consistency despite occlusions and the target leaving the field of view. Through selection, crossover, and mutation, the system learns robust, adaptive policies that actively control viewpoint motion to maintain track, effectively turning the limitation of a narrow FOV into a managed constraint via evolved, purposeful observation behavior.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust Real-Time Vision for Secure Target Detection
ideas.description: This research develops a vision processing system that is robust not only to environmental variability but also to malicious adversarial attacks. It combines adversarially trained neural networks for core detection with a lightweight parallel monitor network that detects anomalies indicative of input manipulation. The system is designed to maintain real-time performance while dynamically assessing its own certainty, allowing it to gracefully degrade or activate defensive protocols under attack. This results in a secure, reliable detection pipeline suitable for safety-critical applications in autonomous systems and surveillance.

ideas.title: Empirical Scaling Laws for Distributed Beamforming Convergence under Real Hardware Impairments
ideas.description: This research direction involves implementing a multi-transmitter hardware testbed to empirically measure the convergence rate of a distributed beamforming algorithm as the number of nodes scales. The core synergy is using the real hardware not just for validation, but as a controlled instrument to discover how specific, measurable impairment levels fundamentally alter the convergence scaling laws. The outcome will be a set of empirically-derived models that predict convergence performance in practical deployments, directly informing system design trade-offs between node count, hardware quality, and required convergence time.

ideas.title: A Hardware-Agnostic Validation Framework Using a Digital Twin of the RF Physical Layer
ideas.description: This research designs and implements a Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) validation framework centered on a high-fidelity digital twin of the impaired RF channel. Instead of building one prototype, the system first uses a reference SDR to capture a diverse library of real-world RF impairments and channel models. This digital twin is then integrated into a testbed that can inject these conditions into the algorithm when it is ported to multiple, distinct candidate hardware platforms. The approach systematically evaluates algorithmic robustness across hardware variability and dynamic environments, providing a more generalizable and rigorous validation than a single prototype test.

ideas.title: Adaptive Signal Denoising for Robust Vision via Neuromorphic Filtering
ideas.description: This research seed proposes improving real-time vision processing robustness under varying lighting and partial occlusion by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, specifically auditory processing. The analogous problem is the robust identification of complex signals (e.g., speech) in acoustically noisy environments, which the auditory cortex solves via hierarchical predictive coding. The adapted idea is to develop a vision algorithm that implements a neuromorphic, predictive filtering mechanism. A higher-level network will continuously generate predictions of the expected visual scene. The core processing will then operate on the 'visual prediction error' signal. This approach inherently dampens the impact of predictable, slow-varying lighting changes (as they become part of the prediction) and amplifies novel features, making target detection more robust to both lighting artifacts and the novel edge patterns introduced by partial occlusions.

ideas.title: Visual Servoing with Integrated Obstacle-Aware Feature Selection and Tracking
ideas.description: This research fuses visual servoing with integrated obstacle avoidance by developing a novel controller that uses a single vision system for both tasks. The core innovation is a dynamic feature management strategy within the visual servoing framework, where the error function for the control law is continuously adapted. It selectively prioritizes visual features of the target that are not occluded by obstacles and incorporates artificial features or constraints from the obstacle boundaries to simultaneously satisfy avoidance criteria. This results in a cohesive navigation policy where tracking and avoidance emerge from a single, optimization-based visual perception-action loop, significantly improving robustness in dense, dynamic scenes compared to layered or switching architectures.

ideas.title: Adapting Swarm Synchronization for Distributed Receive Beamforming
ideas.description: This idea inverts the direction of beamforming by challenging the assumption that distributed receive beamforming requires centralized feedback control. Drawing an analogy from swarm intelligence in biological systems like synchronizing fireflies, where global synchronization emerges from local, pulse-coupled interactions, we adapt this solution to wireless communications. The adapted research idea proposes a decentralized feedback control system for multiple receivers. Instead of a central unit computing and instructing phase adjustments, each receiver acts as an autonomous agent. By observing a common pilot signal or beacon and broadcasting simple timing pulses indicative of its own state, each node iteratively adjusts its phase based on the pulses from neighboring receivers. This local, peer-to-peer feedback mimics pulse-coupled oscillators, enabling the entire distributed array to self-organize and achieve the phase coherence necessary for effective receive beamforming, potentially increasing robustness and scalability.

ideas.title: Robust Distributed Receive Beamforming via Sliding Mode Control for Dynamic Channels
ideas.description: This research synthesizes sliding mode control (SMC) with distributed receive beamforming to create a robust adaptive system for coherent signal reception. The core idea is to treat the phase and gain adjustments at each distributed receiver node as control inputs, with the collective beamforming output as the controlled variable. A sliding mode controller is designed to drive and maintain the system on a 'coherence manifold,' rejecting disturbances from time-varying multipath fading, node mobility, and deliberate jamming. This enables resilient, high-gain reception for applications like distributed sensor arrays or drone swarms in non-stationary and contested electromagnetic environments.

ideas.title: Calibration via Reference Emitters and Blind Gain Estimation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of identical transmitter power by adapting solutions from astrophysical radio interferometry, where arrays of telescopes with heterogeneous, unknown gains must be calibrated. The analogous field solves this using known reference sources (calibrators) and blind self-calibration algorithms that exploit redundancy. The adapted research idea proposes to introduce reference nodes with known transmit powers into a wireless network to calibrate the unknown powers of other nodes. Furthermore, it develops 'blind' estimation frameworks that jointly solve for node locations and their heterogeneous transmit powers by leveraging geometric constraints and signal measurement redundancy, effectively decoupling and estimating the combined 'gain' of each transmitter-receiver path without prior power knowledge.

ideas.title: Apply Biological Motion Parallax Navigation to Visual Servoing for Tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroethology—specifically, how insects use motion parallax for navigation—to the visual servoing tracking problem. Insects like flies solve 3D navigation with 2D visual input by servoing on patterns of optic flow to avoid collisions and follow paths. The adapted research idea proposes a novel 'Optic Flow Servoing' (OFS) framework for robotic tracking. Here, the control law is designed to maintain a desired pattern of optic flow vectors across the image plane, generated by the relative motion between the camera and the target. This method could offer robustness to partial occlusions (as global flow patterns persist) and eliminate the need for explicit 3D reconstruction or precise feature modeling, drawing directly on a proven biological solution for dynamic visual guidance.

ideas.title: Multi-Receiver Distributed Beamforming via Traffic Engineering Principles
ideas.description: This research expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from Network Traffic Engineering in Software-Defined Networking. The analogous problem involves a central controller managing multi-commodity data flows across a shared network with constrained link capacities to serve multiple destinations. The solution involves formulating and solving a global optimization (e.g., convex, linear programming) to allocate resources. Adapted to distributed beamforming, this translates to designing algorithms where a central coordinator or a distributed protocol treats signals for multiple receivers as simultaneous 'flows'. The core challenge is to compute the beamforming vectors across all distributed transmitting nodes to optimally utilize the shared spatial and power resources, aiming to satisfy quality-of-service (QoS) targets for all receivers or maximize network utility for a broadcast channel, thereby relaxing the single-receiver assumption.

ideas.title: Consensus-Driven Blind Source Alignment for Distributed Receive Beamforming
ideas.description: This system enables coherent receive beamforming towards non-cooperative, arbitrary sources by solving the initial alignment problem. It first employs a distributed, computationally efficient blind source separation algorithm across the receiver nodes to identify and geolocate dominant signal components in the environment. These separated component estimates then act as synthetic reference signals to seed a consensus feedback loop, where nodes iteratively adjust their phase weights to constructively combine each target component. The result is a robust, self-initializing framework for distributed receive beamforming that does not require pre-existing cooperation from the signal sources.

ideas.title: Celestial Navigation for Mobile Robots
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical sensing architecture by using a fixed camera network to localize a mobile robot, analogous to celestial navigation where fixed stars locate a ship. The analogous solution from astronomy uses angular measurements to known celestial bodies from an unknown location. Adapted here, the mobile robot is equipped with an upward-facing camera that observes the fixed, uniquely identifiable cameras in the ceiling infrastructure. By applying computer vision to identify these known 'landmark cameras' and calculate their relative bearings and perspectives in its image frame, the robot can triangulate its own precise position and orientation (pose) within the global coordinate frame defined by the stationary camera network, enabling robust guidance.

papers.title: Observability of linear systems under adversarial attacks
papers.abstract:
We address the problem of estimating the state of a differentially flat
system from measurements that may be corrupted by an adversarial attack. In
cyber-physical systems, malicious attacks can directly compromise the system's
sensors or manipulate the communication between sensors and controllers.
